apeiron-het-onbepaalde.kern: Anaximander's answer to the question of the Arche: not water or any other concrete substance, but the indefinite and unlimited — something that possesses no specific quality yet from which all qualities can emerge.
apeiron-het-onbepaalde.uitleg:
Anaximander was a student and successor of Thales of Miletus. Where Thales chooses something perceivable (water) as the primordial principle, Anaximander makes a remarkably more abstract step. His reasoning, insofar as we know it through later sources: if the primal substance possessed one specific quality (warm, cold, wet, dry), how could its opposite then exist in the world? Water cannot produce fire, for water extinguishes fire. The primordial principle must therefore have no quality at all — indefinite, ungrasped, apeiron.

From this indefinite emerge the opposites: warm and cold separate themselves, then dry and wet, and so the ordered world comes into being. When things perish, "they pay penalty to one another for their injustice according to the order of time" — one of the oldest philosophical fragments we possess verbatim.

Russell admires this move. Anaximander shows that he can think one step further than his teacher: he understands that a perceivable principle cannot be the deepest principle. That is an intellectual maneuver that repeats itself throughout history — whenever one assumes that the deepest layer of reality must not be directly perceivable (think of atoms, fields, strings).

At the same time, Anaximander also held more concrete ideas: he imagined the earth as a cylinder floating freely in space (no pillars or turtles needed to support it), and he speculated that life arose from water and that humans descend from fish-like ancestors. For the sixth century BCE, a breathtaking naturalistic package.
apeiron-het-onbepaalde.waarom: The apeiron is the first example of a principle that is essentially invisible yet nonetheless efficacious. That strategy — explaining reality by positing something beyond perception — is central to virtually all later metaphysics and natural science.
apeiron-het-onbepaalde.openVragen:
• Is there a modern analogue to the apeiron? (Quantum fields? The geometry of spacetime?)
  • Why did Anaximenes, the next in the line, choose something concrete again (air)?

arche.kern: Greek for "beginning" or "origin": the one fundamental substance or principle from which everything emerges and to which everything returns. Core question of the Milesian school.
arche.uitleg:
Arche (ἀρχή) is not an answer but a question-form: the presocratics assume that the multiplicity of the world can be traced back to one underlying reality. Thales of Miletus chooses water, Anaximander the indeterminate (apeiron), Anaximenes air.

What these three have in common is more important than their differences: they seek an immanent explanation. Not a divine will that intervenes from without, but a principle that is inherent in nature itself. This is the birthplace of what would later become physics and metaphysics — they are not yet separated here.

Russell sees in this question the first Western attempt to understand the world through reduction: explaining many phenomena from few principles. That move — from multiplicity to unity — remains a red thread running through modern physics, where searches for a Theory of Everything structurally exhibit the same pattern.
arche.waarom: The concept of arche makes visible that even the oldest philosophy already cherished a reductionist ideal. Whoever today searches for the Higgs particle or string theory stands in the same line: suspecting one founding principle behind complexity.
arche.openVragen:
• To what extent is the modern search for fundamental laws of nature truly a continuation of arche-thinking, and to what extent a retroactive projection?
  • Is Anaximander's apeiron (the indeterminate) not philosophically more interesting than a concrete substance like water?

bestuiving-door-hommels.kern: Bumblebees are active under cool, cloudy and humid conditions where honeybees remain inactive, making them indispensable for early spring bloomers and crops in temperate climates.
bestuiving-door-hommels.uitleg:
Bumblebees (Bombus) have three adaptations that make them effective pollinators where honeybees fall short. First, they can vibrate their flight muscles to generate body heat, allowing them to forage at 5–10 °C. Second, some species practise buzz pollination: by vibrating on a flower they shake pollen loose from flowers that don't release it willingly (tomato, eggplant, blueberry). Third, they are hairy and relatively large, allowing them to carry much more pollen per visit.

For a garden this means: early bloomers (crocus, lungwort, willow) are critical for young queens that must establish a nest in spring. Those who want to support bumblebees should provide blooms from March through October, not just in high summer.

The buff-tailed bumblebee (Bombus terrestris) is the most common Dutch species and easily recognized by its two yellow bands and white rear abdomen.
bestuiving-door-hommels.waarom: Practically: much vegetable garden and fruit yield depends on bumblebees, not honeybees. Ecologically: bumblebee populations are under pressure from habitat loss and pesticides, and are a good indicator of garden biodiversity.
bestuiving-door-hommels.openVragen:
• Which native bloomers in my garden zone are most valuable for bumblebees in early spring?
  • How do I distinguish worker, drone and queen in the field?

big-bang.kern: The Big Bang is not an explosion in space, but the beginning of space itself — a state of extreme density and temperature from which the universe began to expand, approximately 13.8 billion years ago.
big-bang.uitleg:
The name "Big Bang" is misleading. There was no point in an existing void that exploded. The universe was that extreme state — everywhere at once. The expansion is not a movement of matter through space, but the stretching of space itself. Imagine the surface of a balloon with dots on it: as you inflate the balloon, all dots move away from each other, but no single dot is the "center" of the expansion.

The evidence for the Big Bang rests on three pillars. The first is redshift: Edwin Hubble discovered in 1929 that distant galaxies are moving away from us, and the farther away, the faster they move. The second is the cosmic microwave background: the afterglow from the moment (±380,000 years after the beginning) when the universe became transparent. The third is the observed ratio of light elements (hydrogen, helium, lithium), which precisely matches what calculations predict for the first minutes after the Big Bang.

What was before the Big Bang is a question that current physics cannot answer — and some physicists argue is not even meaningful. If time itself began at the Big Bang, then "before" is a word without referent. Carroll compares it to asking what lies north of the North Pole.

Sean Carroll emphasizes that the Big Bang is better understood as a condition than as an event: the universe existed in a state of low entropy (high order), and everything that followed — stars, planets, life — is the result of the gradual increase in entropy.
big-bang.waarom: The Big Bang is as close as physics comes to a creation story — but one based on measurable predictions that are confirmed again and again. It is also a demarcation line: everything before ±380,000 years is principally invisible to us via light. What we know about the first seconds comes from particle physics, not from telescopes.
big-bang.openVragen:
• What caused the hypothesized "inflation" — a rapid expansion in the first fraction of a second?
  • Is the universe finite or infinite? The Big Bang says nothing about total size, only about the observable horizon.
big-bang.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 10 (The Edge of Forever): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #10 (Cosmology): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Katie Mack, The End of Everything (2020) discusses five scenarios for how the universe ends

bodemvoedselweb.kern: Beneath every square meter of garden soil lives a community of billions of organisms — bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes, springtails, worms — that together form a food web at least as complex as the visible life above.
bodemvoedselweb.uitleg:
We think of soil as dead material in which plants stand, but healthy soil is a living system. In one teaspoon of garden soil live more micro-organisms than there are people on Earth. These organisms form a food web with clear trophic levels:

At the base are bacteria and fungi. Bacteria break down simple organic matter and make nutrients available to plants. Fungi, including mycorrhizae, do the same but with an added dimension: they form physical networks that transport nutrients across distances bacteria cannot bridge.

One level up live protozoa (single-celled organisms that eat bacteria) and nematodes (microscopic roundworms). When they eat bacteria, they excrete nitrogen in a form plants can directly absorb. This is one of the most important mechanisms of natural fertilization — no artificial fertilizer needed; the system regulates itself, provided you don't disturb it.

Another level up: springtails, mites, earthworms, and millipedes. Earthworms are the most familiar: they mix organic material through the soil, create aeration, and their droppings (worm castings) are among the richest natural fertilizers.

The health of this web largely determines how well plants grow, how resistant they are to disease, and how much water the soil holds. A disturbed soil food web (from tilling, artificial fertilizer, pesticides) produces plants that become dependent on external input — comparable to a person on antibiotics who loses their gut flora.
bodemvoedselweb.waarom: It changes what "fertilizing the soil" means. Instead of giving nutrients to plants, you feed the web that feeds the plants. Compost, mulch, fallen leaves — they are not waste but fuel for the underground ecosystem.
bodemvoedselweb.openVragen:
• How quickly does a disturbed soil food web recover after you stop tilling?
  • Are there simple indicators (visible fungal threads, earthworm density) by which you can assess the health of your soil web?
bodemvoedselweb.verder_lezen:
• Merlin Sheldrake, Entangled Life (2020) — on fungi as the invisible architects of ecosystems
  • Jeff Lowenfels & Wayne Lewis, Teaming with Microbes (2010) — a practical book on soil biology for gardeners

compost-als-ecosysteem.kern: A compost heap is not a waste bin but a managed ecosystem in which billions of organisms work together to break down complex organic molecules into plant nutrients and stable humus.
compost-als-ecosysteem.uitleg:
Composting seems simple: throw plant scraps on a pile and wait. But what happens in that pile is an orchestrated process that unfolds in phases, comparable to ecological succession.

The first phase (mesophilic, 20-40°C) is dominated by bacteria and fungi that break down simple sugars and proteins. Their metabolism generates heat — the pile warms up by itself. In the second phase (thermophilic, 40-70°C), heat-resistant bacteria take over. At 55-65°C, pathogens, weed seeds, and parasites are killed. This is the "sterilizing" phase that makes compost safe for the garden.

After weeks, the pile cools and the maturation phase begins. Now the true architects appear: fungi that break down cellulose and lignin (woody material), springtails, mites, woodlice, and eventually earthworms. They transform the material into humus — a dark, crumbly, sweet-smelling substance that retains water, binds nutrients, and forms the ideal growing medium for mycorrhizal networks.

Interesting is the parallel with Entropy: at first glance, composting seems the opposite of entropy increase. You start with disorderly waste and end with structured humus. But local order (humus formation) is made possible by greater disorder elsewhere — the heat the pile releases to the environment is entropy increase. It is the same principle as photosynthesis: local order at the cost of global disorder.

The carbon-to-nitrogen ratio (C/N ratio) is the most important parameter. Brown material (leaves, wood, cardboard) is carbon-rich. Green material (grass clippings, kitchen waste, manure) is nitrogen-rich. A ratio of approximately 25-30:1 is optimal. Too much carbon and the process stalls; too much nitrogen and the pile begins to smell (anaerobic decomposition, ammonia formation).
compost-als-ecosysteem.waarom: Composting is the most direct way to feed the Soil Food Web. And it makes you aware of cycles: what comes out of the garden (leaf fall, prunings) does not need to be "removed" but is the raw material for next year.
compost-als-ecosysteem.openVragen:
• Is bokashi (fermentation instead of composting) better or worse for the soil food web?
  • How does worm compost (vermicompost) differ from thermophilic compost in terms of microbial life?
compost-als-ecosysteem.verder_lezen:
• Lowenfels & Lewis, Teaming with Microbes (2010) — the microbiology of compost explained for gardeners
  • Sheldrake, Entangled Life (2020) — the role of fungi in decomposition processes

de-drake-vergelijking.kern: A formula that estimates how many detectable civilizations could exist in our galaxy by breaking down the problem into seven factors — from the rate at which stars form to the lifespan of technological civilizations.
de-drake-vergelijking.uitleg:
Frank Drake formulated the equation in 1961, not as a definitive answer but as a framework for conversation. The equation reads: N = R* × fp × ne × fl × fi × fc × L. Each factor is a link in the chain from "star" to "civilization we can communicate with":

R* is the rate at which stars form. fp is the fraction of stars with planets. ne is the average number of habitable planets per star system. fl is the fraction where life actually emerges. fi is the fraction where intelligent life evolves. fc is the fraction that develops technology to transmit signals. L is the average lifespan of such a civilization.

The first three factors are now reasonably well understood thanks to the Kepler and TESS space telescopes: star formation is well-modeled, most stars have planets, and rocky planets in the habitable zone are surprisingly common.

But the last four factors — the biological and sociological ones — remain completely unknown. We have exactly one example of life emerging (Earth), one example of intelligence, one example of technology. Whether that is typical or exceptional, we simply do not know.

Sagan addresses the Drake Equation extensively in Cosmos (Ch. 12) and was personally optimistic about it. He estimated N at millions of civilizations in our galaxy alone. But he emphasized that the most uncertain factor is L — the lifespan of civilizations. A civilization that destroys itself (through nuclear weapons, climate change, or something we cannot imagine) contributes hardly anything to N, no matter how large the other factors are.

This makes the Drake Equation not only a scientific but also an existential formula: the question "are we alone?" is inseparably linked to the question "do we survive?"
de-drake-vergelijking.waarom: It is one of the rare equations that brings cosmology, biology, and sociology together in a single formula. And it shows that the answer to "are we alone?" depends not only on how the universe works, but on how civilizations treat themselves.
de-drake-vergelijking.openVragen:
• If the James Webb Space Telescope finds biosignatures in the atmosphere of an exoplanet, which factors in the Drake Equation would then be constrained?
  • Is the Fermi Paradox ("if there should be so many civilizations, where is everyone?") evidence that L is very short?
de-drake-vergelijking.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 12 (Encyclopaedia Galactica): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Al-Khalili (ed.), Aliens: The World's Leading Scientists on the Search for Extraterrestrial Life (2016)

de-nachtelijke-hemel-lezen.kern: The starry sky is not a static backdrop but a legible map of cosmic distances, stellar lives, and terrestrial seasons — and virtually anyone can learn to read it with the naked eye.
de-nachtelijke-hemel-lezen.uitleg:
On a clear night, away from city lights, roughly 2,500 stars are visible to the naked eye. That feels like a lot, but it is a fraction of a fraction: our galaxy contains an estimated 200-400 billion stars.

What you see is not a snapshot but a temporal mosaic. The light from Sirius (8.6 light-years away) is traveling for more than eight years. The light from Betelgeuse (±700 light-years) departed in the late Middle Ages. The faint blur of the Andromeda Galaxy — the most distant object visible to the naked eye — shows light that was emitted 2.5 million years ago, when our ancestors were making their first stone tools. The sky is literally a glimpse into the past, and that past grows deeper the further you look.

The seasonal change of the starry sky occurs because the Earth orbits the Sun. In winter (in the Netherlands), you look outside at night, away from the Sun, toward Orion and the winter constellations. In summer you look the other way, toward the center of the Milky Way (Sagittarius, Scorpius). That is why the Milky Way band is visible in summer and not in winter.

Reference points to start with: the Big Dipper (always visible above the Netherlands, circling the Pole Star), Orion (winter, three stars in a row as a "belt"), and the Milky Way band (summer nights, from southeast to northwest as a hazy streak).

Pythagoras saw in celestial motions the ultimate confirmation of his philosophy: the planets move in mathematical proportions, like music you cannot hear but can calculate. Sagan describes in Cosmos how Greek philosophers looked at the same sky as we do and took the first steps toward a rational understanding of it — away from mythology, toward astronomy.

Light pollution is the primary obstacle. In the Gooi region, dark spots are becoming increasingly rare, but the Naardermeer and the edges of the Veluwe still offer reasonably good observation conditions.
de-nachtelijke-hemel-lezen.waarom: The night sky connects daily life with cosmology. Every star you see is a nuclear fusion reactor (Stellar nucleosynthesis), every color difference tells something about temperature and life stage, and the fact that the sky is dark despite billions of stars is itself a profound cosmological given (Olbers' paradox).
de-nachtelijke-hemel-lezen.openVragen:
• Why is the night sky dark if the universe is full of stars? (Olbers' paradox — the answer touches on the Big Bang and the finiteness of the universe in time)
  • Which planets are currently visible in the evening sky above the Netherlands?
de-nachtelijke-hemel-lezen.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 1 (The Shores of the Cosmic Ocean) and Ch. 7 (The Backbone of Night): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 5 (The Theory of Gravitation): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_07.html
  • Observatory Naarden/Bussum — local observation evenings

democritus-en-het-atomisme.kern: Democritus proposed that all things consist of indivisible particles — atomos ("uncuttable") — moving through empty space. Combinations and configurations of atoms explain all diversity in the world.
democritus-en-het-atomisme.uitleg:
Atomism is a brilliant synthesis of two opposing positions. Parmenides claimed that change is impossible. Heraclitus claimed that everything constantly changes. Democritus (building on his teacher Leucippus) resolved the tension: the atoms themselves never change — they are eternal, indivisible, and unchangeable, exactly as Parmenides' Being demanded. But their configurations change constantly — they move, collide, interlock and separate, and this explains the flux that Heraclitus observed.

All perceivable properties — colour, taste, temperature — are not truly intrinsic to things themselves, but the result of how atoms are arranged and how they act upon our senses. "By convention there is sweet and bitter, hot and cold, colour; but in reality there are only atoms and void." This is a staggeringly modern insight for the 5th century BCE.

Russell is outright enthusiastic about Democritus — he calls him the most scientific thinker of all Greek antiquity. The atomistic hypothesis is not merely a philosophical position but an anticipation of modern chemistry and particle physics. The core idea — invisible, indivisible particles whose configurations explain the visible world — is precisely what Dalton rediscovered in the 19th century and what Rutherford confirmed experimentally in the 20th.

Feynman opens Six Easy Pieces with the statement that if you were allowed to pass on only one sentence to future generations, that sentence should be: "everything is made of atoms." It is the same intuition as Democritus, but now grounded in 2400 years of evidence.

Al-Khalili's BBC documentary Atom follows precisely this line: from Democritus' philosophical speculation through Dalton's chemistry to Rutherford's gold foil experiment, and ultimately to the Standard Model. The title of the series is a tribute to the idea that began 2400 years ago.
democritus-en-het-atomisme.waarom: Atomism is the most powerful example of a purely philosophical idea that was later confirmed scientifically. Not because Democritus was right for the right reasons (he had no experimental evidence), but because the structure of his thinking — reduce complexity to simple building blocks and their interactions — became the blueprint for all modern natural science.
democritus-en-het-atomisme.openVragen:
• Modern "atoms" are indeed divisible (into protons, neutrons, electrons, quarks). Are quarks then the true "atomos"? Or is divisibility potentially infinite?
  • Why was Democritus' atomism largely overshadowed in antiquity by Plato and Aristotle, who rejected it?
democritus-en-het-atomisme.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 9: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 1 (Atoms in Motion): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_01.html
  • Al-Khalili, Atom (BBC Four, 2007, Part 1: The Clash of the Titans) — available on YouTube
  • Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 7 (The Backbone of Night): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf

ecologische-successie-in-een-tuin.kern: Every garden is a frozen stage in a natural process: if you stop intervening, bare soil evolves through herbs and grasses toward shrubs and eventually forest. Understanding where your garden sits in that sequence changes how you garden.
ecologische-successie-in-een-tuin.uitleg:
Succession is the process whereby living communities follow one another in a place. On bare ground, pioneer species appear first: fast-growing annual herbs (nettle, ragwort, chickweed) that need plenty of light and produce seed quickly. They stabilize the soil and add organic matter.

After a few years, biennials and perennials take over: they are slower but more competitive. They shade out the pioneers and bring more structure. Then come shrubs (bramble, elder, hawthorn), and eventually — if you let it run for decades — trees. The final stage in the Netherlands is typically deciduous forest, dominated by oak and beech.

A garden is an artificially frozen stage. By mowing, pruning, and weeding, you hold succession at a particular point: a lawn is permanently grassland, a border is permanently herb-stage. There is nothing wrong with that, but it does explain why gardening costs labor: you are constantly fighting the system's natural tendency to evolve further.

The opposite — working with succession rather than against it — is the basis of much ecological gardening. Letting a wild corner become overgrown, creating a hedgerow, leaving dead wood: you create different stages alongside each other, and with them habitat diversity. More stages = more niches = more species = more pollinators, more birds, more soil organisms.

Mycorrhizal networks benefit greatly from non-disturbance: in a garden with permanent planting (fruit trees, perennials) the networks are thicker and more effective than in a garden dug over annually.
ecologische-successie-in-een-tuin.waarom: It changes your perspective on "weeds." A nettle is not an enemy but a pioneer species doing what it should do: colonizing bare patches and building soil. Whether you want it depends on where you want to position your garden in the succession sequence.
ecologische-successie-in-een-tuin.openVragen:
• Which successional stages are richest in biodiversity in an average Dutch backyard?
  • How long does it take before an overgrown corner of 10m² forms a stable microhabitat?
ecologische-successie-in-een-tuin.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 2 (One Voice in the Cosmic Fugue) — evolution as a cosmic selection process, comparable to ecological succession on a smaller scale
  • Dave Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — practical book on wild garden ecology

eenheid-der-tegendelen.kern: Heraclitus' doctrine that opposites are not truly separate but two sides of the same process: cold and warmth, day and night, life and death exist only in relation to each other.
eenheid-der-tegendelen.uitleg:
For Heraclitus, reality is fundamentally tensioned. Not in the sense of being conflictual, but in the sense of a bowstring: the string can only function thanks to the tension between two opposing forces. Remove that tension and the bow is useless.

Examples he gives: the path upward and the path downward are the same path. Sea water is simultaneously beneficial (for fish) and deadly (for humans). Sickness makes health valuable; hunger makes satiation pleasant.

This is more than poetic observation. Heraclitus claims that the logos — the ordering principle of reality — actually works through opposites. The cosmos is harmony, but harmony that arises from tension, not from monotony. "War is the father of all things" is his most provocative formulation.

Russell sees in this a precursor to Hegel's dialectics (thesis-antithesis-synthesis), though he warns that Heraclitus was less systematic than Hegel would later make it. The core is older and rougher: not a neatly constructed philosophical system, but a direct intuition about how the world fits together.

In modern physics, this echoes in the concept of symmetry breaking: the fundamental laws are symmetric, but the observable world exists precisely because that symmetry is broken. Matter and antimatter, positive and negative charge — the structure of reality rests on opposites.
eenheid-der-tegendelen.waarom: It is a radical alternative to the idea that harmony means rest, stillness, or unity. Heraclitus claims that harmony is dynamic and requires conflict. That changes how you look at everything — from ecosystems (predator-prey) to music (dissonant-consonant) to social relations.
eenheid-der-tegendelen.openVragen:
• To what extent is symmetry breaking in physics truly an echo of Heraclitus, or are we projecting retroactively?
  • How does Heraclitus' "harmony from tension" differ from the Chinese yin-yang concept?
eenheid-der-tegendelen.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 4: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Sean Carroll, Biggest Ideas #11 (Symmetry): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/

entropie.kern: Entropy is a measure of the number of ways a system can be arranged without you noticing the difference. The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of an isolated system always increases — the world moves from order to disorder.
entropie.uitleg:
Imagine a box with a dividing wall: all red balls on the left, all blue balls on the right. That is a state with low entropy — there is only one way to achieve precisely that configuration. Remove the wall and the balls mix. There are enormously many more ways to arrange them mixed than sorted, so the mixed state is overwhelmingly more probable. Entropy increase is not a mysterious force but simply statistics: there are more disordered states than ordered ones.

Feynman emphasizes that this does not mean local order is impossible. Stars form, crystals grow, living organisms build complex structures — but always at the cost of greater disorder elsewhere. Your refrigerator makes the inside colder, but produces heat on the back. Net entropy of the whole increases.

Carroll connects entropy to the deepest question of cosmology: why did the universe have such low entropy at the Big Bang? If high entropy is the "natural" state, then the early universe was extremely unnatural — extraordinarily ordered, like a freshly shuffled deck of cards that happened to turn out in sequence. Everything that happened afterward — stars forming, planets cooling, life evolving — is the gradual unwinding of that original order.

This also makes entropy the reason why time has a direction. The past is the end with lower entropy, the future the end with higher entropy. We remember the past but not the future, because memory itself is a low-entropy phenomenon.
entropie.waarom: Entropy connects everyday experience (coffee goes cold, iron rusts, rooms become messy) with the deepest structure of the universe. It is also one of the few physical laws that is asymmetric in time — almost all other equations work just as well backward as forward.
entropie.openVragen:
• If the universe evolves toward maximum entropy, is the end station the "heat death" — a uniform, lukewarm, structureless void?
  • Why was entropy so low at the Big Bang? Nobody knows. Carroll calls this the most fundamental unsolved problem in cosmology.
entropie.verder_lezen:
• Carroll, Biggest Ideas #19 (Entropy): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 4 (Conservation of Energy): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_04.html
  • Carroll, From Eternity to Here (book, 2010) — fully devoted to entropy and the arrow of time

fotosynthese-als-kosmisch-proces.kern: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert sunlight into chemical energy. But at a deeper level, it is something more remarkable: it is the mechanism through which starlight is converted into life on Earth.
fotosynthese-als-kosmisch-proces.uitleg:
The sun is a nuclear fusion reactor that converts hydrogen into helium and radiates photons (light particles) in the process. Those photons travel eight minutes through space and reach a leaf. In that leaf, chlorophyll captures the photon and uses the energy to split water molecules and build CO₂ from the air into sugars. Oxygen is the byproduct.

That sounds like a biology lesson, but Sean Carroll shows that it is actually a lesson in entropy. The sun emits high-energy photons (few photons, much energy per unit). The Earth radiates the same total energy back into space, but as many more photons each with less energy. The difference — few photons in, many photons out — is precisely what entropy increase means. Plants tap into that flow: they capture the ordered, energy-rich photons and use them to create local order (sugars, cell structures, growth), while the net result is still entropy increase.

Every food chain on Earth — from the soil food web to the bumblebee on the lavender to the bread on your plate — is ultimately powered by this one process. The energy in a mushroom comes from the wood it breaks down, which came from the tree, which came from photosynthesis, which came from the sun, which comes from nuclear fusion. It is an unbroken chain from the core of a star to your plate.

Sagan calls this the reason why we are "stardust" in an even deeper sense than merely chemical composition: not only are we made of stellar material, we are also powered by starlight. Without the sun, photosynthesis would stop, and within weeks almost all complex life on Earth would vanish.

Recent research in quantum biology (Al-Khalili, Life on the Edge) suggests that photosynthesis may use quantum effects to make energy transfer in the leaf extremely efficient — the photon "tastes" multiple pathways simultaneously, as it were, to find the fastest route to the reaction center.
fotosynthese-als-kosmisch-proces.waarom: Photosynthesis connects three domains of knowledge: cosmology (the sun as energy source), nature (plants and ecosystems), and even philosophy (the arche question: what ultimately drives everything?). It is one of the most powerful examples of how seemingly disconnected phenomena are connected at the deepest level.
fotosynthese-als-kosmisch-proces.openVragen:
• How much of the energy that a garden plant absorbs through photosynthesis ultimately ends up in the soil food web?
  • Are the quantum effects in photosynthesis actually functional, or a byproduct that evolution has not "noticed"?
fotosynthese-als-kosmisch-proces.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 2 (One Voice in the Cosmic Fugue): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #19 (Entropy) — explanation of how the sun provides the Earth with free energy: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Al-Khalili & McFadden, Life on the Edge (2014), Ch. 4 — quantum photosynthesis
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 4 (Conservation of Energy): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_04.html

heraclitus-en-de-logos.kern: Heraclitus of Ephesus held that everything is in constant flux ("panta rhei"), yet behind this change lies a rational ordering principle: the logos.
heraclitus-en-de-logos.uitleg:
Where Thales of Miletus and Anaximander sought a fundamental substance (arche), Heraclitus shifts the question: not what is everything made of, but how does everything hold together? His answer is the logos — a word that can mean "reason," "word," "proportion," and "law" all at once. The logos is the principle that ensures change is not arbitrary but follows a pattern.

The most famous image is the river: you cannot step into the same river twice, because the water is always different. But the river as a river persists — precisely because of the constant flow. Stability emerges from change, not despite it.

For Heraclitus, fire is the best symbol of reality: it is always in motion, constantly transforming, yet follows laws. The world is "an eternal living fire, that kindling in measures and going out in measures."

Russell values Heraclitus as the first to place process at the center rather than substance. That is a fundamentally different orientation from the Milesians, and one that is fully recovered only in modern physics (thermodynamics, quantum field theory).
heraclitus-en-de-logos.waarom: The tension between change and stability is one of the deepest questions in both philosophy and physics. Heraclitus chooses radically for change as primary — a position that resonates in modern physics with the idea that reality at its deepest level consists of processes and interactions, not fixed "things."
heraclitus-en-de-logos.openVragen:
• How does Heraclitus' logos relate to modern natural laws? Is it the same intuition?
  • Why does Parmenides take precisely the opposite stance (nothing ever changes)?
heraclitus-en-de-logos.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 4: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Al-Khalili's Atom (BBC, Part 1) touches on the same tension between change and permanence in atomic physics

het-dubbelspletexperiment.kern: Fire particles one by one through two slits in a screen, and they produce an interference pattern as if they were waves — even when each particle is fired individually. It is the experiment that shows the quantum world obeys no classical intuition whatsoever.
het-dubbelspletexperiment.uitleg:
Imagine this: you shoot bullets at a wall with two openings. Behind the wall stands a detector. You expect two stripes on the detector — one behind each opening. That's correct for bullets.

Now you do the same with water waves. Waves pass through both openings at once and interfere with each other: where two wave crests coincide, reinforcement occurs; where a crest and a trough coincide, cancellation occurs. The result is a pattern of alternating strong and weak bands — an interference pattern.

The shocking part: if you fire electrons (or photons, or even molecules) one by one through the two slits, the pattern on the detector gradually builds up into... an interference pattern. Not two stripes, but wave-like bands. Each particle "knows" about both slits somehow, even though it only passed through one.

Feynman called this the experiment that "contains the heart of quantum mechanics," and that "is impossible, absolutely impossible, to explain in a classical way." It forces us to accept that a particle does not behave as a particle *or* a wave, but as something for which we have no everyday analogy.

Even stranger: if you place a detector at one of the slits to "watch" which slit the particle goes through, the interference pattern disappears and you get two stripes — the bullet pattern. The act of observation itself changes the result.

Al-Khalili uses this experiment as a thread running through his book Quantum and his BBC documentary Atom. He emphasizes that this is not about a technical limitation of our measuring apparatus, but about something fundamental: the quantum world *is* different from the world we see with our eyes.
het-dubbelspletexperiment.waarom: It is the sharpest proof that reality at the smallest scale differs radically from our everyday experience. Democritus imagined atoms as tiny marbles. They are not. They are something fundamentally stranger — and the double-slit experiment is the place where that strangeness becomes most clearly visible.
het-dubbelspletexperiment.openVragen:
• What is the particle between the moment it is fired and the moment it arrives at the detector? Quantum mechanics says: don't ask that question, or accept that it has no sensible answer.
  • What does Feynman mean when he says that "nobody understands quantum mechanics"?
het-dubbelspletexperiment.verder_lezen:
• Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 6 (Quantum Behavior): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_37.html
  • Al-Khalili, Quantum: A Guide for the Perplexed (2003) — the experiment as a unifying thread
  • Al-Khalili, Atom (BBC Four, 2007, Part 2: The Key to the Cosmos) — available on YouTube
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #13 (Quantum Mechanics): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/

kosmische-achtergrondstraling.kern: Weak microwave radiation coming from all directions of the universe, fossil light from the period approximately 380,000 years after the Big Bang, when the universe first became transparent.
kosmische-achtergrondstraling.uitleg:
In the early universe, matter was a hot, opaque soup of free protons and electrons — photons could not travel far without colliding. After approximately 380,000 years, the universe had cooled sufficiently (±3000 K) that electrons could bind to nuclei to form neutral hydrogen. Suddenly light could travel unimpeded. We observe that light today, but stretched by the expansion of the universe to microwave wavelengths — amounting to a black-body spectrum of approximately 2.7 K.

The discovery in 1964 by Penzias and Wilson (as unexplained noise in an antenna) provided the decisive empirical support for the Big Bang model over the steady-state alternative. Later measurements (COBE, WMAP, Planck) revealed extremely subtle temperature variations — the seeds from which galaxies grew.
kosmische-achtergrondstraling.waarom: The cosmic microwave background is the most distant "photograph" we can make of the universe. Everything behind it is in principle invisible to electromagnetic radiation — you would need neutrinos or gravitational waves. It is literally our horizon in time.
kosmische-achtergrondstraling.openVragen:
• What do the patterns in the CMB tell us about a possible inflation period?
  • How does the "cold spot" in the CMB relate to models of the early universe?

mycorrhiza.kern: Symbiotic relationship between fungi and plant roots in which the fungus supplies water and nutrients in exchange for sugars from photosynthesis. More than 90% of all land plants live in such a relationship.
mycorrhiza.uitleg:
A plant root appears at first glance to be the complete underground system, but this is misleading. Around virtually every root grows a network of fungal filaments (hyphae) that are many times thinner and longer than the root itself. These hyphae explore the soil volume that the plant alone could never reach and extract phosphate, nitrogen, water, and trace elements from it.

In return, the plant supplies up to 20-30% of its photosynthesis production to the fungus in the form of sugars. That sounds like a lot, but the exchange is extremely advantageous: plant productivity in a mycorrhizal relationship is often twice as high as without it.

There are two main types: endomycorrhiza penetrates the plant cells and is by far the most common (grasslands, herbs, most garden crops). Ectomycorrhiza wraps around the roots from the outside and is associated with trees such as oak, beech, and pine — this is what brings mushrooms to the surface in a forest. That mushroom is not the entire fungus, merely its fruiting body.

A less well-known consequence: fungal networks often connect multiple plants at the same time. Via this "wood wide web" sugars and even defensive signals can be exchanged between trees. The implications of this are still the subject of active research.

Practically for a garden: plowing, digging, and synthetic fertilizers disrupt mycorrhizal networks. No-dig gardening and compost as a soil improver keep the network intact.
mycorrhiza.waarom: It changes what "the plant" is. A tree is actually always a tree-plus-fungal-network, and separating the two denies the reality of how life above and below ground is actually organized.
mycorrhiza.openVragen:
• To what extent does bokashi or compost promote mycorrhizal formation compared to doing nothing?
  • Are there signs in your own garden (for example, mushrooms in autumn) that ectomycorrhiza is active?

parmenides-en-het-onveranderlijke-zijn.kern: Parmenides of Elea argued that change and motion are logically impossible: only Being is, and it cannot come into being, perish, or change. The senses that show us change deceive us.
parmenides-en-het-onveranderlijke-zijn.uitleg:
Parmenides takes the exact opposite position from Heraclitus. Where Heraclitus sees everything in motion, Parmenides claims that genuine change is a logical contradiction. His argument: if something comes into being, it either comes from Being (but then it already existed) or from Non-Being (but nothing can come from nothing). Therefore, coming-into-being is impossible. The same holds for perishing.

What remains is Being: unchanging, indivisible, timeless, like a perfect sphere. The world of change and diversity we perceive is mere appearance — the "way of opinion" versus the "way of truth."

Russell calls this one of the most important moments in Western philosophy. Parmenides forces, for the first time, the distinction between what reason tells us and what the senses show us — and chooses radically for reason. This move reappears in Plato (the theory of Forms), in Descartes (systematic doubt), and in modern mathematics (formal proof over intuition).

His student Zeno devised the famous paradoxes (Achilles and the tortoise, the flying arrow) precisely to defend Parmenides: if motion truly existed, it would lead to absurdities — therefore Parmenides is right.

The tension between Heraclitus and Parmenides (everything changes vs. nothing changes) has never been fully resolved. All subsequent Greek philosophy can be read as attempts to reconcile these two extremes — Plato's theory of Forms, Aristotle's potency and actuality, the Atomists with their unchanging atoms in changing configurations.
parmenides-en-het-onveranderlijke-zijn.waarom: Parmenides' argument sounds absurd (we do see movement, don't we?), but the logical structure is impeccable. It forces you to choose: do you trust reason or the senses when they conflict? That question has remained current — quantum mechanics describes a reality that our senses cannot grasp, yet is mathematically consistent.
parmenides-en-het-onveranderlijke-zijn.openVragen:
• Is the Standard Model of particle physics — with its unchanging laws and changing configurations — a modern synthesis of Parmenides and Heraclitus?
  • Why has Parmenides' purely logical approach exerted such influence on Plato?
parmenides-en-het-onveranderlijke-zijn.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 5: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Al-Khalili, Paradox, treats Zeno's paradoxes as direct heirs to Parmenides

pythagoras-en-het-getal-als-werkelijkhei.kern: Pythagoras and his followers claimed that numbers are not merely descriptions of reality, but reality itself. Everything is number.
pythagoras-en-het-getal-als-werkelijkhei.uitleg:
After the Milesians who sought a primary substance (Arche) and Heraclitus who placed change at the center, Pythagoras offers a radically different answer: the deepest layer of reality is not material but mathematical. The world is structured by numerical ratios, and those ratios are what the world is.

The key moment was a discovery in music. Pythagoras (or his school) discovered that harmonious musical intervals correspond to simple numerical ratios: an octave is 2:1, a fifth 3:2, a fourth 4:3. When you halve a string, it sounds the same tone one octave higher. Musical beauty turned out to have mathematical structure.

From this, the Pythagoreans generalized: if music follows numerical ratios, perhaps everything does? The orbits of planets, the structure of matter, the harmony of the cosmos — everything is reducible to number and proportion. The famous "harmony of the spheres" is the idea that planets in their orbits produce an inaudible music, dictated by mathematical ratios.

Russell emphasizes the enormous influence of this step. The Pythagoreans are the first to propose a non-material Arche — not water, air, or fire, but abstract mathematical structure. That is a forerunner of Plato's theory of forms and, surprisingly, of modern theoretical physics. When Galileo wrote that "the book of nature is written in the language of mathematics" he was essentially repeating Pythagoras.

But there was also a crisis. The discovery that the square root of 2 cannot be written as a fraction (an irrational number) was a shock that undermined the Pythagorean system. If everything is number, but some ratios cannot be expressed as number, then the foundation wavers. According to legend, Hippasus, who made this discovery public, was drowned by his fellow members.
pythagoras-en-het-getal-als-werkelijkhei.waarom: Modern physics is profoundly Pythagorean: reality turns out to behave according to mathematical patterns that we discover, not invent. The periodic table, quantum mechanics, relativity theory — they are mathematical structures that nature "follows." Whether nature actually is what mathematics describes, or whether mathematics is merely our best descriptive language, is a question that remains open.
pythagoras-en-het-getal-als-werkelijkhei.openVragen:
• Is the modern discussion of the "mathematical universe" (Max Tegmark) a direct heir to Pythagoras?
  • What does the discovery of irrational numbers mean for the idea that everything is number?
pythagoras-en-het-getal-als-werkelijkhei.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 3: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 7 (The Backbone of Night) — discusses Pythagoras in the context of Greek astronomy: https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf

roodverschuiving.kern: The phenomenon whereby light from a receding object shifts toward longer (redder) wavelengths. For galaxies, this shift is evidence that the universe is expanding.
roodverschuiving.uitleg:
The basic principle is the same as the Doppler effect you hear from a passing ambulance: the pitch gets lower as the ambulance moves away. Something analogous happens with light — when two objects move away from each other, the light waves are "stretched" and the spectrum shifts toward red.

For stars within our own Milky Way galaxy, this is simply the Doppler effect due to their motion. But for distant galaxies, something more fundamental is at play: it is not so much that they are moving away from us through space, but that space itself is expanding between us and them. The light waves stretch along with that space.

Edwin Hubble discovered in 1929 that the farther a galaxy is from us, the greater its redshift. This linear relationship (Hubble's law) is the empirical foundation for the idea of an expanding universe, and thus for Big Bang theory.

Extreme redshift makes it possible to look back in time. A galaxy with z=7 (z is the formal redshift parameter) is seen as it was when the universe was only 750 million years old. The Cosmic microwave background is the extreme case: z≈1100, corresponding to 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
roodverschuiving.waarom: Redshift is not merely a nice spectral phenomenon but literally our cosmic measuring tape and clock all in one. Everything we say about distance and temporal depth in the universe rests on this measurement.
roodverschuiving.openVragen:
• How do astronomers distinguish cosmological redshift (due to space expansion) from Doppler redshift (due to proper motion) and gravitational redshift (light escaping a gravitational well)?
  • What does it mean that the observed expansion is accelerating? (Dark energy, a concept yet to be covered)

stellaire-nucleosynthese.kern: Nearly all elements heavier than hydrogen and helium were forged in the cores of stars or in their violent death. The carbon in your body, the iron in your blood, the calcium in your bones — it is all stardust.
stellaire-nucleosynthese.uitleg:
After the Big Bang, the universe consisted almost entirely of hydrogen (±75%) and helium (±25%), with traces of lithium. That is not enough to build planets, oceans, or living beings. Everything heavier — carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, iron, gold — had to be made elsewhere.

That "elsewhere" is stars. In a star's core, light atomic nuclei are compressed into heavier ones, a process called nuclear fusion. The sun is currently fusing hydrogen into helium. Heavier stars go further: helium into carbon, carbon into oxygen, oxygen into silicon, and eventually silicon into iron. At iron, the process stops — fusing iron costs more energy than it yields.

Elements heavier than iron (copper, silver, gold, uranium) are only created in the violent end of massive stars: supernovae. The shockwave of such an explosion provides enough energy to forge even the heaviest elements, and then blasts them into space, where they become part of gas clouds from which new stars and planets form.

Sagan summed it up in what is perhaps his most famous sentence: "We are made of stardust." It is not a metaphor. It is literally the case: the periodic table of elements is a catalog of stellar deaths.
stellaire-nucleosynthese.waarom: It connects the smallest (atomic nuclei) with the largest (stellar lives and deaths). And it provides a stunning answer to the question of where we come from: not from the earth, but from stars that died before the earth existed. The ancient question of the Arche — what is everything made of? — receives here a modern answer.
stellaire-nucleosynthese.openVragen:
• Recently, neutron star collisions have also been identified as a source of heavy elements. How does that compare to supernovae as a dominant source?
  • If all iron on Earth comes from supernovae, how many stars would have been needed to provide enough material for our solar system?
stellaire-nucleosynthese.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 9 (The Lives of the Stars): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 1 (Atoms in Motion): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_01.html
  • Al-Khalili, Chemistry: A Volatile History (BBC, part 1) covers the discovery of elements

tuinvogels-als-indicator-voor-biodiversi.titel: Garden birds as an indicator of biodiversity
tuinvogels-als-indicator-voor-biodiversi.kern: The bird species you see in your garden tell you more about the ecological health of your garden than almost any other observation. The more species, the more complex the food web beneath.
tuinvogels-als-indicator-voor-biodiversi.uitleg:
Birds stand at the top of the garden food web. A great tit consumes tens of thousands of caterpillars per nesting period. A blackbird lives on earthworms, snails, and berries. A woodpecker hunts for larvae in dead wood. Every bird species you see implies an entire chain beneath it: insects, soil organisms, plants that feed those insects.

This is why the number of bird species is a quick indicator. A garden with only great tits and sparrows has a basic food web. A garden that also attracts wrens, robins, nuthatches, greenfinches, and coal tits has a rich and layered ecosystem: dense shrubland (wren), open soil with leaf litter (robin), old trees with bark insects (nuthatch), seed plants (greenfinch), and varied tree canopies (coal tit).

A few practical principles that follow directly from this logic:

Structural variation is everything. Birds need not only food but also nesting sites and shelter. A garden with lawn, borders, and a wild corner with shrubs attracts more species than a neat garden of the same size.

Native plants outperform exotics. A native hawthorn harbors over 200 insect species; an exotic conifer almost none. More insects = more food for insect-eating birds.

Dead wood is living wood for biodiversity. A dead tree trunk or brush pile is habitat for beetles, larvae, fungi, and the birds that hunt them. Tidying up is ecologically the worst thing you can do.

Vogelbescherming Nederland organizes the National Garden Bird Count annually (late January). Participating is not only enjoyable but gives you a personal dataset over the years of how your garden ecosystem develops.
tuinvogels-als-indicator-voor-biodiversi.waarom: Birds are the visible top of an invisible pyramid. Observing them is not only relaxing but directly informs you about the health of the soil food web, the quality of your plantings, and the success of your ecological garden management.
tuinvogels-als-indicator-voor-biodiversi.openVragen:
• Which species should I expect in my specific garden and surroundings (Bussum/Gooi)?
  • How long does it take before a change (planting a hedge, hanging nest boxes) becomes visible in the number of bird species?
tuinvogels-als-indicator-voor-biodiversi.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 2 (One Voice in the Cosmic Fugue) — evolution and biodiversity as a cosmic phenomenon: https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Dave Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — the ecology of a backyard
  • Vogelbescherming Nederland: https://www.vogelbescherming.nl/tuinvogeltelling

vroege-voorjaarsbloeiers.kern: Plants that flower from February to April, when few other nectar sources are available. Critical for young bumblebee queens that must establish a nest after hibernation.
vroege-voorjaarsbloeiers.uitleg:
When a bumblebee queen crawls out of her winter shelter in March, she has one task: find food quickly enough to start a nest. If she fails, the colony dies before it begins. Early blooming is therefore not decoration but infrastructure.

The most valuable species for a Dutch garden in this period are crocus and snowdrop in February/March, followed by lungwort, grape hyacinth and ramsons, and later in April the goat willow (Salix caprea) — one of the most important sources for bees and bumblebees because it provides enormous quantities of pollen precisely when little else is available.

Notably, many gardens have a "gap" between the snowdrops of February and the perennials that only begin in May. In those gap weeks from mid-March to mid-April, pollinators are most vulnerable. Whoever aligns their garden with this period accomplishes more ecologically than someone who wants abundant colour in summer.

Practical advice: plant bulbs in autumn, consider a native willow as a shrub or tree form, and be restrained about clearing "weeds" in March — dead-nettle, dandelion and mouse-ear are surprisingly valuable during this period.
vroege-voorjaarsbloeiers.waarom: Every pollinated flower in April translates later into berries, fruits, seeds. The summer garden is a function of what was available to pollinators in the early spring.
vroege-voorjaarsbloeiers.openVragen:
• Which native early bloomers thrive on my soil type?
  • Is there a practical way to combine the willow with other garden functions (e.g. as a standard tree or pollard form)?

water-als-oerstof.kern: Thales of Miletus' thesis that water is the fundamental principle (arche) from which everything exists or originates.
water-als-oerstof.uitleg:
Thales left no systematic argumentation that has survived, but Aristotle later speculates about his reasons: water is necessary for life, seeds are moist, food contains moisture, and water appears in three states of matter (ice, water, vapor) — which is an example of one substance taking different forms.

It is tempting to read this as naïve. But Russell points out that we must see it differently: Thales did something radically new by asking at all what everything is made of, and by trying to answer that without invoking gods. That he chose water instead of "the breath of Zeus" or "primordial sound" is remarkably modern.

Note that Thales' answer is closer to observation than that of his successor Anaximander (the apeiron, the boundless). There lies a tension running through all early Greek philosophy: do you choose something concrete that you can point to, or something abstract that is logically more complete?
water-als-oerstof.waarom: The specific hypothesis is outdated, but the type of explanation — monism with an observable basis — remains a recurring motif. Compare it with the idea that everything consists of atoms, or of energy, or of information.
water-als-oerstof.openVragen: • Did Thales have concrete water in mind (H₂O in our terms), or "the moist" as a broader category?

zenos-paradoxen.kern: Zeno of Elea devised a series of thought experiments intended to prove that motion is logically impossible — in defense of his teacher Parmenides.
zenos-paradoxen.uitleg:
The most famous is Achilles and the Tortoise. Achilles runs ten times as fast as a tortoise and gives it a 100-meter head start. By the time Achilles has covered 100 meters, the tortoise has moved 10 meters further. When he covers that 10 meters, the tortoise is 1 meter ahead again. And so on — there is always a remaining distance. Achilles seems never to catch the tortoise.

A second paradox: the flying arrow. At any single moment, the arrow is at rest (it occupies exactly the space corresponding to its size). But if it is at rest at every moment, when does it move?

Zeno's point is not that motion appears to exist — he does not deny that. His point is that motion cannot be logically understood if you assume that space and time are infinitely divisible. And if logic says something is impossible while your senses say it exists, then you must — like Parmenides — choose logic.

Russell devotes more attention to Zeno than to almost any other Presocratic philosopher, because the paradoxes were not truly resolved until the 19th century through the mathematical theory of limits and convergent series. The sum 100 + 10 + 1 + 0.1 + ... converges to a finite number (111.111...), and Achilles catches the tortoise after precisely that distance. But the fact that Zeno's question waited 2400 years for an answer shows how deeply it cut.

Al-Khalili treats Zeno's paradoxes in Paradox as one of the nine greatest enigmas in physics, connecting them to the modern question of whether space and time are continuous (infinitely divisible) or discrete (composed of smallest units, as the Planck length suggests).
zenos-paradoxen.waarom: The paradoxes touch on a fundamental question that remains open: is reality continuous or discrete? Quantum mechanics works with discrete energy levels, but spacetime in Einstein's relativity theory is continuous. That tension — Zeno's legacy — is one reason why we still lack a working theory of quantum gravity.
zenos-paradoxen.openVragen:
• If space and time ultimately prove to be discrete (quantum gravity), will Zeno have won after all?
  • How does the mathematical solution (limits) differ from a physical solution (is motion really what we think it is)?
zenos-paradoxen.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 6: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Al-Khalili, Paradox: The Nine Greatest Enigmas in Physics (2012) — Zeno as the first paradox
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #4 (Space) — the question of whether space is continuous or discrete: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/

zwaartekracht-en-lokale-orde.kern: Gravity is the force that gives the universe structure — stars, planets, galaxies — while simultaneously increasing total entropy. Local order does not arise in spite of the second law of thermodynamics, but because of it.
zwaartekracht-en-lokale-orde.uitleg:
The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of a closed system always increases. But look around you: stars form from gas clouds, planets condense from dust disks, crystals grow, life evolves. Structure emerges everywhere. How can that be?

The answer lies in two insights that together complete the picture.

Insight 1: the Earth is not a closed system. It continuously receives high-energy sunlight and radiates low-energy heat back into space. That difference is a stream of free energy that enables local ordering — plants that grow, animals that move, brains that think — while total entropy (Earth plus surroundings) neatly increases. All life on Earth is a byproduct of that energy stream, as described in Photosynthesis as a Cosmic Process.

Insight 2: with gravity, "clumpy" is precisely the high-entropy state. This is the counterintuitive part. For gas in a box: evenly distributed = high entropy, clumped together = low entropy. But for matter under the influence of gravity, it is reversed. A perfectly uniform gas cloud is gravitationally unstable — it is an extremely improbable state that cannot persist long. The "natural" end state is that everything clumps together into the most compact form possible: a black hole, surrounded by thermal radiation.

This explains why the universe shortly after the Big Bang — when matter was almost perfectly uniformly distributed — had a state of extremely low entropy. It looked "messy" (hot soup, everything the same), but gravitationally speaking it was exceptionally ordered: nothing had yet clumped together. Everything that happened afterwards — gas clouds collapsing, stars forming, planets condensing, black holes growing — is the universe evolving toward higher entropy.

When a gas cloud collapses into a star, enormous amounts of heat are released. That heat radiates away as vast numbers of low-energy photons. The entropy of all those photons is much greater than the entropy decrease of the ordered star. On balance, entropy increases, exactly as the second law prescribes.

Sean Carroll summarizes it: gravity is the engine that drives the universe from smooth to clumpy, and that clumpying is entropy increase. The structures we observe — galaxies, solar systems, planets — are not exceptions to the second law. They are the second law in action.
zwaartekracht-en-lokale-orde.waarom: It resolves one of the most persistent misconceptions about entropy: that order "is not allowed" to arise. Order not only may arise locally, it must arise locally as the universe as a whole moves toward higher entropy. Stars, planets, and life are not miracles that defy physics — they are the inevitable byproducts of a universe that cools and clumps.
zwaartekracht-en-lokale-orde.openVragen:
• If the high-entropy end state of the universe is a collection of black holes that eventually evaporate (Hawking radiation), what remains? Only photons in an ever-expanding void?
  • Carroll calls the low entropy at the Big Bang the most fundamental unsolved problem in cosmology. Is there an explanation for why the universe began this way, or is it simply a boundary condition we must accept?
zwaartekracht-en-lokale-orde.verder_lezen:
