  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #10 (Cosmology): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Carroll, From Eternity to Here (2010) — complete book on entropy, the arrow of time, and the low entropy of the Big Bang
  • Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 9 (The Lives of the Stars) — star formation as an order-creating process: https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 4 (Conservation of Energy) — energy conservation and thermodynamics: https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_04.html

zwarte-gaten.kern: A black hole is a region in space where gravity is so strong that nothing — not even light — can escape. It is not a "thing" in space, but a property of spacetime itself: a zone from which return is impossible.
zwarte-gaten.uitleg:
When a massive star (more than approximately 20 times the mass of the sun) exhausts its fuel and can no longer sustain nuclear fusion, there is no force left to resist gravity. The core collapses to a point of (theoretically) infinite density: a singularity. Around that singularity forms the event horizon — the boundary within which the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light.

That horizon is not a physical surface. You can fall through it without noticing anything unusual at that moment. But once inside, you cannot escape the singularity, just as you cannot escape the future within the universe — the singularity is the future for everything that falls within the horizon.

Carroll explains that black holes are essentially a prediction of Einstein's general relativity: if mass curves spacetime, then enough mass curves spacetime so strongly that it closes in on itself. It is not an exotic addition to the theory but an inevitable consequence of it.

For a long time, black holes were purely theoretical. The first indirect observation was Cygnus X-1 in the 1970s — an invisible object that was pulling matter from a nearby star. In 2019, the Event Horizon Telescope made the first direct "photograph" of a black hole (M87*): a dark shadow surrounded by a ring of glowing gas.

The deepest mystery is the information problem. According to quantum mechanics, information can never be lost. But if matter falls into a black hole and the black hole eventually evaporates (Hawking radiation), where has the information gone? This is one of the sharpest tensions between the two great theories of physics — and it remains unsolved.
zwarte-gaten.waarom: Black holes are the places where our two best theories (relativity and quantum mechanics) come into conflict. They are not merely cosmic curiosities, but the battleground where the next fundamental breakthrough in physics is expected.
zwarte-gaten.openVragen:
• What is "inside" a black hole? Is the singularity real, or does the theory simply break down there?
  • How can Hawking radiation preserve information if the black hole evaporates?
zwarte-gaten.verder_lezen:
• Carroll, Biggest Ideas #9 (Black Holes): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Al-Khalili, Black Holes, Wormholes and Time Machines (1999) — accessible book specifically on this subject
  • Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 9 (The Lives of the Stars) — addresses the final stages of massive stars: https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf

aristoteles-en-de-vier-oorzaken.kern: Aristotle held that you truly understand something only when you know four kinds of causes: what it is made of (material), what structure it has (formal), what set it in motion (efficient), and what it is for (final). That last one — the purpose — is the most controversial and influential.
aristoteles-en-de-vier-oorzaken.uitleg:
Take an oak wooden table. The material cause is the wood. The formal cause is the design — four legs, a top, certain proportions. The efficient cause is the carpenter who made it. The final cause is the purpose: to lay things on, to eat at.

So far this sounds like common sense. But Aristotle applies this schema to nature itself. An acorn grows into an oak — the final cause of the acorn is to become an oak. Rain falls so that plants can grow. Everything in nature has a purpose, a telos.

This is teleological thinking: explaining the world in terms of purposes. It stands in direct opposition to Democritus, who explained everything through blind mechanical interactions of atoms — no purpose, no plan, only collisions. Russell observes that the entire history of Western science can be read as a slow victory of Democritus over Aristotle: modern physics and biology are fundamentally mechanistic, not teleological.

But Aristotle's influence was enormous and long-lasting. Through Arabic philosophers (Avicenna, Averroës) and Thomas Aquinas, his thinking dominated medieval philosophy and theology. The "final cause" fit perfectly with the Christian idea of purposeful creation.

Russell is ambivalent. He admires Aristotle's systematic mind — his logic, his biology, his political philosophy — but finds teleology an intellectual brake that delayed science for centuries. Only when Galileo and Newton showed that you could describe nature without purposes did modern science get underway.
aristoteles-en-de-vier-oorzaken.waarom: Teleological thinking is deeply human — we are purpose-seeking beings and project that onto the world. The question of whether nature is truly purposeless (Democritus, modern science) or purposeful (Aristotle) touches on the heart of how we relate to the cosmos. And in biology the question is subtle: evolution has no purpose, but it produces organisms that look purposefully designed.
aristoteles-en-de-vier-oorzaken.openVragen:
• Is evolutionary biology secretly teleological? We say "eyes are for seeing" — is that Aristotelian thinking in disguise?
  • Is the final cause making a comeback in cosmology? Some physicists (Carroll among them) ask why the initial conditions of the universe were precisely such that complexity could arise.
aristoteles-en-de-vier-oorzaken.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 19-22: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Al-Khalili, Science and Islam (BBC, 2009) — Aristotle through the Arabic philosophers

bokashi-en-composteren-twee-processen-ee.titel: Bokashi and composting — two processes, one cycle
bokashi-en-composteren-twee-processen-ee.kern: Bokashi and composting are fundamentally different biological processes — fermentation versus aerobic decomposition — but they work best as two phases in the same cycle: bokashi as pre-digestion, composting as completion.
bokashi-en-composteren-twee-processen-ee.uitleg:
Composting is aerobic: it works with oxygen and a broad community of bacteria, fungi, springtails, mites, and earthworms. Temperatures rise to 65°C. The end product is humus — stable, pH-neutral to slightly alkaline, directly usable by the soil food web.

Bokashi is anaerobic: it works without oxygen, in a sealed bucket. The "effective microorganisms" (EM) on the bokashi bran are primarily lactic acid bacteria, yeasts, and phototrophic bacteria — a much narrower community. They ferment the material rather than decompose it, similar to making sauerkraut. The end product is not compost but fermented organic material: acidic (pH 3-4), still recognizable in structure, and not directly usable for plants.

The magic lies in the combination. When fermented bokashi material goes onto an active compost heap, three things happen simultaneously.

First: decomposition accelerates. The fermented material is already "pre-digested" — the cell structures have been broken down by lactic acid bacteria, making it easier for aerobic organisms to access. Bokashi material composts noticeably faster than fresh kitchen waste. The lactic acid bacteria die off once exposed to oxygen (they are obligately anaerobic), but the work they have done remains.

Second: the pH shock is temporary. The bokashi material is acidic (pH 3-4), but an active compost heap has enormous buffering capacity. Aerobic bacteria and fungi neutralize the acid within days to weeks. As long as bokashi makes up no more than 20-30% of the heap volume, acidification is not a problem. The heap produces its own alkaline byproducts — ammonia from nitrogen decomposition, calcium carbonate from eggshells — that compensate for the acids.

Third: the soil food web benefits. The lactic acids are a food source for certain soil organisms. The moist, half-digested material quickly attracts earthworms. And the combination delivers a richer end product than composting alone, because fermentation makes additional nutrients available — especially phosphorus and potassium.

There is one point to watch. If you add a lot of bokashi at once to a small, cold, poorly active heap, you can temporarily create an overly acidic environment that drives earthworms away — worms are sensitive to pH below 5. The solution is simple: mix with structural material (leaves, wood chips) and optionally add a handful of garden lime or eggshells as a buffer.

The real strength of bokashi is that it accepts kitchen waste that causes problems on an ordinary heap: cooked food, meat, fish, dairy. Fermentation suppresses rotting odors and kills most pathogens. On an ordinary compost heap, meat attracts rats and stinks; via bokashi it is safely and odorlessly pre-digested before going on the heap.

The cycle then becomes: kitchen waste → bokashi bucket (2-3 weeks anaerobic fermentation) → compost heap (months of aerobic decomposition) → humus → soil → mycorrhizal networks → plant roots → food → kitchen waste. A closed loop, driven by two complementary biological processes.
bokashi-en-composteren-twee-processen-ee.waarom: It is one of the few places where you as a gardener consciously combine the two fundamental strategies of life — fermentation and respiration. Fermentation is the oldest metabolism on Earth (3.5 billion years old, from before oxygen entered the atmosphere). Aerobic decomposition is younger and more efficient. Bokashi → compost is essentially the evolution of life repeated in your backyard.
bokashi-en-composteren-twee-processen-ee.openVragen:
• Does the microbial composition of the end product change measurably if you add bokashi versus pure composting?
  • How does the nutritional value of bokashi-enriched compost compare to pure compost in a controlled garden trial?
  • Is the claim that EM microorganisms improve soil health scientifically supported, or is it mainly marketing?
bokashi-en-composteren-twee-processen-ee.verder_lezen:
• Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — composting and the microbiology of decomposition
  • Lowenfels & Lewis, Teaming with Microbes (2010) — aerobic versus anaerobic processes in soil
  • Sheldrake, Entangled Life (2020) — the role of fungi in decomposition processes: https://archive.org/details/entangled-life_202203

de-bibliotheek-van-alexandrie.kern: The Library of Alexandria was the greatest knowledge center of the ancient world — a place where all known human knowledge was gathered, copied, and studied. Its gradual destruction is a symbol of the fragility of civilization and knowledge.
de-bibliotheek-van-alexandrie.uitleg:
The Library was founded around 300 BCE by Ptolemy I, one of Alexander the Great's successors, in the Egyptian port city of Alexandria. At its height, it contained an estimated 400,000–700,000 book rolls — the majority of all written knowledge in the Greek, Egyptian, Persian, and Babylonian world.

It was more than a storehouse. The Library was a research institute: scholars were paid to work, debate, and produce new scholarship there. Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Earth (with only 2% error). Aristarchus proposed the heliocentric model — 1,800 years before Copernicus. Euclid wrote his Elements there. Hipparchus catalogued the stars. It was the closest approximation to a university that the ancient world knew.

Sagan devotes an entire chapter of Cosmos to the Library and describes its destruction as one of the greatest intellectual catastrophes in human history. The details of the destruction are historically unclear — it was probably not a single fire but a gradual decline over centuries, accelerated by political unrest, religious conflict, and neglect. What is clear: most of the work of the Presocratics, of Democritus, of hundreds of other thinkers, has been lost. We know their ideas only through fragments and summaries by later authors.

Russell's treatment of Greek philosophy illustrates this loss indirectly: almost everything he writes about the Presocratics comes from secondary sources — Aristotle citing Thales, Simplicius citing Anaximander. The original works have vanished.

Sagan draws a line to the present: knowledge that is not actively maintained, copied, and transmitted disappears. That was true for papyrus rolls in antiquity and is true for digital data today. The Library of Alexandria is not merely history but a warning.
de-bibliotheek-van-alexandrie.waarom: It reminds us that progress is not inevitable. A civilization can lose knowledge, and that loss can take centuries to recover from — if it is recovered at all. The Dark Ages following the fall of Rome are partly a consequence of the loss that began in Alexandria.
de-bibliotheek-van-alexandrie.openVragen:
• How differently would we understand the Presocratics if their original works had been preserved?
  • Is the internet our modern Library of Alexandria — and is it equally fragile?
de-bibliotheek-van-alexandrie.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 11 (The Persistence of Memory): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I — the dependence on secondary sources for Presocratic philosophy: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Al-Khalili, Science and Islam (BBC, 2009) — how Arabic translators rescued parts of lost knowledge

de-driehoek-noethereinsteinbohr.kern: Between 1905 and 1927, three revolutions took place simultaneously in physics: Einstein's relativity, Bohr's quantum mechanics, and Noether's symmetry framework. They are rarely discussed together, but Noether's work is the mathematical glue that binds the other two — and explains why they clash.
de-driehoek-noethereinsteinbohr.uitleg:
The three storylines run parallel in time but are seldom told as a single whole.

Einstein (1905–1915) discovers that space and time are not absolute but elastic — they warp with velocity (special relativity, 1905) and with mass (general relativity, 1915). But his theory creates a problem: in a curved, dynamic spacetime it is no longer self-evident what "energy conservation" means. If space itself changes, where does the energy go? Einstein brings this problem to the mathematicians in Göttingen.

Noether (1915–1918) tackles that problem and not only solves it but discovers something far deeper. Her theorem proves that energy conservation is a consequence of time symmetry — the assumption that the laws of nature work the same tomorrow as they do today. In general relativity, that symmetry is subtly broken: the universe expands, the "rules" on cosmic scales shift, and therefore energy on the scale of the whole universe is not strictly conserved. Einstein's intuition was correct: there was indeed something strange about energy. Noether laid bare precisely what.

Bohr (1913–1927) meanwhile revolutionizes the physics of the smallest scales. His atomic model (1913) introduces discrete energy levels — electrons can only occupy certain orbits, not everything in between. Heisenberg formulates in 1927 the uncertainty principle: you cannot know both the position and momentum of a particle exactly at the same time. The same holds for energy and time.

Here the connection with Noether becomes deep. Those "pairs" that Heisenberg discovers — position/momentum, energy/time — are not arbitrary. Noether's theorem explains precisely why these quantities are coupled to each other:

  • Position and momentum are coupled because momentum arises from spatial symmetry (the laws are the same at every place). Position is the variable that belongs to that symmetry.
  • Energy and time are coupled because energy arises from time symmetry (the laws are the same at every moment). Time is the variable that belongs to that symmetry.

The uncertainty principle is thus not an accidental limitation of our measuring apparatus. It is a mathematically inevitable consequence of the symmetry structure of nature — the structure that Noether laid bare.

What bringing this together means: Einstein showed that space and time are dynamic. Bohr showed that energy and matter are discrete. Noether showed that the laws governing both worlds are rooted in symmetry — and that when that symmetry breaks, the familiar conservation laws go with it.

The great unsolved question of modern physics — how do you combine Einstein's gravity (continuous, smooth spacetime) with Bohr's quantum mechanics (discrete, probabilistic matter)? — is essentially the question of which symmetries remain at the deepest level. Noether's framework is the language in which that answer will be formulated, if it is ever found.
de-driehoek-noethereinsteinbohr.waarom:
The three revolutions are often taught separately: relativity in one course, quantum mechanics in another, and Noether is skipped. But without Noether the story misses its red thread. She is the one who shows that conservation laws, uncertainty relations, and the structure of spacetime are not three separate facts but three expressions of the same principle: symmetry.

It is also a story about whom we remember. Einstein is an icon. Bohr is known to anyone who touches physics. Noether — whose work is at least as fundamental — you have to look up. That she was a woman at a time when women were not admitted to universities explains the oblivion but does not justify it.
de-driehoek-noethereinsteinbohr.openVragen:
• If quantum gravity is ever found, what symmetry underlies it? And what conservation law belongs with it?
  • Carroll argues that the expansion of the universe breaks time symmetry and thereby renders energy conservation invalid on cosmic scales. How far do the consequences of that reach?
  • Noether's theorem applies to continuous symmetries. Quantum mechanics also knows discrete symmetries (such as matter/antimatter). How do those relate to Noether?
de-driehoek-noethereinsteinbohr.verder_lezen:
• Carroll, Biggest Ideas #1 (Conservation) — how conservation laws follow from symmetry: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #11 (Symmetry) — Noether's theorem in detail: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 4 (Conservation of Energy) — energy conservation as empirical fact, without the "why" that Noether provides: https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_04.html
  • Al-Khalili, Atom (BBC Four, 2007) — the parallel development of relativity and quantum mechanics
  • Dwight E. Neuenschwander, Emmy Noether's Wonderful Theorem (2010) — accessible explanation of the theorem for non-mathematicians

de-egel-als-tuinbewoner.kern: The hedgehog is the only wild mammal most Dutch people regularly encounter in their gardens, and simultaneously one of the fastest declining. The way you garden determines whether it can survive.
de-egel-als-tuinbewoner.uitleg:
The European hedgehog (Erinaceus europaeus) is a nocturnal animal that feeds on insects, slugs, earthworms, beetles, and occasionally a frog or mouse. An adult hedgehog eats per night the equivalent of a third of its body weight — making it one of the most effective natural pest controllers in a garden.

But the hedgehog population in the Netherlands has declined sharply over recent decades. The main causes are not the usual suspects (pesticides, climate) but something more mundane: habitat fragmentation. Hedgehogs have a home range of 2-5 hectares and travel up to 2 kilometers per night. They must be able to move between gardens, parks, and green strips. Every impenetrable fence, every closed garden boundary is a wall in their world.

The simplest and most impactful measure a garden owner can take is a "hedgehog gate": a hole of 13×13 cm at the bottom of the fence or gate. It is large enough for a hedgehog but too small for most cats. If neighbors do the same, a network of connected gardens emerges — a hedgehog highway through the neighborhood.

Furthermore, hedgehogs depend on untidiness. A neat garden with short grass, no leaf piles, and no shrubbery offers no shelter, no overwintering spot, and little food. What a hedgehog needs: a leaf pile or brush pile to shelter and overwinter in, a compost heap (full of worms and beetles), no robot mower at night (lethal), and no slug pellets (poisoning through the food chain — the hedgehog eats the poisoned slug).

The hedgehog is a so-called "umbrella species": if you make your garden suitable for hedgehogs, you automatically make it suitable for a whole range of other species — toads, solitary bees, earthworms, soil insects. The hedgehog is the indicator that the whole system works.
de-egel-als-tuinbewoner.waarom: The hedgehog is the most visible and recognizable representative of garden biodiversity. A garden where a hedgehog lives is a garden where the soil food web functions, where insects provide sufficient food, and where the structure has enough variation. It is also the species that is easiest to help: one hole in the fence can make the difference.
de-egel-als-tuinbewoner.openVragen:
• How do I know if hedgehogs visit my garden? (Signs: droppings, sounds, wildlife camera)
  • Is supplementary feeding with cat food (often recommended) really beneficial, or does it disrupt natural foraging behavior?
de-egel-als-tuinbewoner.verder_lezen:
• Hedgehog Working Group Netherlands: https://www.egelbescherming.nl/
  • Dave Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — hedgehogs in the garden ecosystem
  • National Hedgehog Monitoring (RAVON/Zoogdiervereniging)

donkere-energie.kern: The universe is not only expanding — that expansion is accelerating. The mysterious force responsible for this, we call dark energy. It makes up approximately 68% of the total energy content of the universe, and we have no idea what it is.
donkere-energie.uitleg:
In 1998, two independent teams of astronomers discovered something bewildering. They studied distant supernovae (exploding stars of a type whose brightness is precisely known) to measure how fast the universe is expanding. The expectation was that the expansion would slow down — gravity, after all, pulls everything together. But the supernovae were dimmer than predicted, which meant they were farther away than expected: the universe is not expanding more slowly but ever faster.

It's as if you throw a ball upward and instead of falling back down, it accelerates ever faster upward. Something must be pushing the ball — something stronger than the gravitational force pulling it back.

We call that "something" dark energy, but the name is essentially a label for our ignorance. The simplest explanation is the cosmological constant — a property of empty space itself that exerts a constant outward pressure. Einstein introduced this constant originally in 1917 to make a static universe possible, later called it his "greatest blunder" when Hubble discovered the universe was expanding, but it turned out he was ironically correct after all — just for a different reason.

Carroll explains how strange the situation is: if you try to calculate the cosmological constant from quantum mechanics, you get a value that is 10^120 times too large. That is the biggest discrepancy between prediction and measurement in the entire history of science. There is clearly something fundamental we do not understand.

Together with dark matter, dark energy delivers a sobering message: of all the energy and matter in the universe, only 5% consists of the ordinary matter we know — atoms, stars, planets, you. The rest is dark.
donkere-energie.waarom: Dark energy determines the fate of the universe. If the acceleration continues, galaxies will move away from each other ever faster until they disappear beyond the cosmic horizon. The observable universe will become increasingly empty, colder, and lonelier. Whether another scenario is possible depends on the nature of dark energy — and we do not know that.
donkere-energie.openVragen:
• Is dark energy truly constant, or does it change over time? (DESI measurements from 2024 suggest possibly the latter)
  • Is the dark energy problem connected to the problem of quantum gravity? Both point to something fundamental we are missing.
donkere-energie.verder_lezen:
• Carroll, Biggest Ideas #10 (Cosmology): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Katie Mack, The End of Everything (2020) — five scenarios for the end of the universe, each dependent on dark energy
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 5 (The Theory of Gravitation): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_07.html

donkere-materie.kern: Approximately 85% of all matter in the universe is invisible — it emits no light, absorbs no light, and does not interact with electromagnetic radiation. We know it exists because we observe its gravitational effects. What it is, we do not know.
donkere-materie.uitleg:
The problem first became clear in the 1930s when Fritz Zwicky measured the velocities of galaxies in the Coma cluster. They were moving far too fast to stay bound together — as if there were much more mass than you could see. Zwicky called it "dunkle Materie." For decades he was ignored.

In the 1970s, Vera Rubin confirmed the same problem at a different scale. She measured the rotation speeds of stars in spiral galaxies and discovered that stars at the edge rotate just as fast as stars near the center. That is strange: if only the visible matter were present, outer stars should move much more slowly (just as Neptune orbits the sun more slowly than Mercury). The rotation pattern only made sense if each galaxy is surrounded by an invisible halo of extra mass.

Since then, the evidence has become overwhelming. The cosmic microwave background shows patterns that only make sense if dark matter exists. The way galaxies bend light from background objects (gravitational lensing) reveals mass that "shouldn't be there." The large-scale structure of the universe — how galaxies are distributed in filaments and clusters with empty voids between them — can only be reproduced in simulations that include dark matter.

What it is not: it is not black holes, not cold gas clouds, not undetected stars. These options have been systematically ruled out. It must be something we do not yet know — a new type of particle that interacts with ordinary matter only through gravity.

Carroll emphasizes that this is not a minor detail. The matter from which you, the Earth, the Sun, and all stars are made comprises only 15% of all matter. We are the byproduct; dark matter is the main course.
donkere-materie.waarom: It is one of the biggest open questions in physics. We have a universe in which we cannot identify 85% of the matter. At the same time, dark matter is essential for the existence of galaxies — without it, gravity would not produce enough clustering to form structures. We exist thanks to something we do not understand.
donkere-materie.openVragen:
• Are there alternative theories of gravity (MOND) that make dark matter unnecessary, or do they fail under careful testing?
  • If dark matter is a particle, why have particle accelerators not found it yet?
donkere-materie.verder_lezen:
• Carroll, Biggest Ideas #10 (Cosmology): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 10 (The Edge of Forever): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Carroll, Mindscape podcast — multiple episodes on dark matter

entropie-symmetrie-en-de-structuur-van-h.titel: Entropy, Symmetry, and the Structure of the Universe
entropie-symmetrie-en-de-structuur-van-h.kern: The history of the universe is a cascade of symmetry-breakings, driven by the increase of entropy. Each time the universe cools and entropy rises, a symmetry breaks and new structure emerges. Everything we know — forces, matter, stars, life — is the product of that double movement.
entropie-symmetrie-en-de-structuur-van-h.uitleg:
This concept connects three threads developed separately in the vault: Entropy, Noether's Theorem, and Symmetry Breaking — why imperfection makes the world. Together they tell one story.

Entropy is not a conservation law. That is the first crucial point. Energy is conserved — Noether's theorem derives that from time symmetry. Momentum is conserved — that follows from spatial symmetry. But entropy is not conserved. It increases. The Second Law of Thermodynamics is the only fundamental law that points in a direction: forward, never backward. Entropy does not belong to a symmetry — it belongs to the breaking of one.

The fundamental laws are time-reversible. The equations of Newton, Einstein, and quantum mechanics work identically forward and backward in time. At the level of the laws, there is perfect time symmetry. Two billiard balls colliding look just as plausible played backward as forward.

But reality is not time-reversible. Eggs break and do not restore themselves. Coffee cools and does not spontaneously warm up. The universe expands and does not contract. The arrow of time points only one way. How can an asymmetric result arise from symmetric laws?

The answer is the initial condition. The laws themselves do not dictate that entropy increases — they say it is overwhelmingly probable, given that the universe began in a state of extremely low entropy. The Big Bang was a state of near-perfect uniformity — extremely hot, all particles equivalent, no structure. That sounds "disordered," but gravitationally speaking, uniformity is actually the most improbable, most ordered state. The low entropy of the Big Bang is the boundary condition that drives everything.

Carroll emphasizes: the time symmetry is not broken by the laws but by the specific initial condition. It is spontaneous symmetry breaking on the largest possible scale — the universe "chose" a starting point, and everything after follows from it.

What happens next is a cascade. As the universe cools and entropy rises, symmetries break in sequence:

10⁻³⁶ seconds: The grand unified force splits. The strong nuclear force separates. The symmetry between three of the four fundamental forces breaks.

10⁻¹² seconds: The Higgs field freezes out. Particles acquire mass. The electroweak symmetry breaks. Suddenly electromagnetism and the weak force are two different phenomena.

1 second to 3 minutes: Protons and neutrons form the first atomic nuclei (hydrogen, helium). The symmetry between free quarks and bound nuclei breaks.

380,000 years: The universe cools to ~3000 K. Electrons bind to nuclei — neutral atoms appear. Light can suddenly travel freely. The cosmic microwave background is the fossil of this moment.

Millions to billions of years: Gas clouds clump through gravity. The spatial uniformity — the high symmetry of the early universe — breaks into structure. Stars form, fuse light elements into heavy ones, explode, and scatter their material. Planets condense. Chemistry becomes possible.

After billions of years: On at least one planet, life arises. Organisms build local order — cells, organs, brains — at the cost of greater disorder in their surroundings. Photosynthesis captures the flow of free energy from the sun and drives the entire biosphere. Everything — from the soil food web to human consciousness — is an eddy in the river of increasing entropy.

Each step is the same movement: entropy increases, a symmetry breaks, and structure appears that was not there before. The laws do not change — only the state changes, and each new state has less symmetry but more complexity than the previous one.

The paradox hidden in this is that "disorder" and "structure" are not opposites when gravity is in play. For gas in a box: uniform = high entropy. But for matter under gravity, the opposite holds: clumpy = high entropy. Stars and galaxies are not exceptions to the Second Law — they are the Second Law in action, under the influence of gravity.

The final state — if we extend the line — is maximum entropy: a universe of black holes slowly evaporating into photons in an ever-expanding, ever-colder void. All symmetries broken, all structure gone, all possibilities exhausted. From potency to act, until there is nothing left to actualize.
entropie-symmetrie-en-de-structuur-van-h.waarom:
This is the story of everything. Not as metaphor but literally: physics describes the universe as a one-time transition from maximum symmetry to maximum entropy, and along the way, everything that is appears. We find ourselves somewhere in the middle of that transition — far enough that there are stars and planets and life, but not so far that everything has already burned out.

It also connects all three domains of the vault. Philosophy (Aristotle's potency/act, Heraclitus' becoming reality), cosmology (Big Bang, symmetry breaking, dark energy), and nature (photosynthesis, soil food web, ecological cycles) are not three separate areas of interest but three perspectives on the same process: a universe that creates structure by breaking symmetry, driven by the relentless increase of entropy.
entropie-symmetrie-en-de-structuur-van-h.openVragen:
• Why did the universe begin with low entropy? No one knows. Carroll calls this the most fundamental unsolved problem in all of physics. Is it a chance initial condition, or does it follow from a deeper law we have not yet discovered?
  • If the universe reaches maximum entropy, is that truly the end? Or can a fluctuation in the maximum-entropy state "start" a new universe with low entropy again? (Boltzmann brain hypothesis)
  • Is life an inevitable consequence of entropy increase in a universe with the right initial conditions, or a chance byproduct?
entropie-symmetrie-en-de-structuur-van-h.verder_lezen:

  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #11 (Symmetry) — Noether and symmetry breaking: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Carroll, From Eternity to Here (2010) — the complete story of entropy, time, and cosmology
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 4 (Conservation of Energy): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_04.html

epicurus-en-het-verlangen-naar-rust.titel: Epicurus and the Desire for Tranquility
epicurus-en-het-verlangen-naar-rust.kern: Epicurus taught that the highest good is not pleasure in the conventional sense, but ataraxia — peace of mind, the absence of fear and pain. Happiness is not the pursuit of pleasures but the elimination of unnecessary desires.
epicurus-en-het-verlangen-naar-rust.uitleg:
Epicurus (341–270 BCE) is often misunderstood as a hedonist in the modern sense — someone who calls for excessive eating, drinking, and feasting. The opposite is true. He lived soberly, ate mainly bread and water, and considered a piece of cheese a luxury. His point was not that you should pursue everything pleasant, but that you must learn to distinguish which desires are natural and necessary (food, shelter, friendship), which are natural but not necessary (fine food, comfort), and which are neither natural nor necessary (fame, power, wealth). The last category is the source of nearly all unhappiness.

Philosophically, Epicurus built upon Democritus: everything exists of atoms and void, the soul is material and perishes with the body, and there is no afterlife. That sounds bleak, but Epicurus turns it around: precisely because death is nothing—"when we exist, death is not present; when death is present, we do not exist"—we need not fear it. And precisely because there are no divine punishments awaiting, we can free ourselves from fear and focus on what truly matters: tranquility, friendship, and the simple pleasure of existence.

Russell sets Epicurus against the Stoics: both seek peace of mind, but the Stoic finds it in accepting fate, while the Epicurean withdraws from public life into a community of friends ("the Garden"). Russell respects Epicurus' honesty but finds his philosophy too withdrawn—there is no room in it for public engagement or political courage.

Yet Epicurus' influence on the modern world is greater than often acknowledged. His materialism—no supernatural explanations needed—is a forerunner of the scientific outlook. And his focus on the elimination of unnecessary suffering—rather than the pursuit of positive pleasures—sounds remarkably modern in light of psychology.
epicurus-en-het-verlangen-naar-rust.waarom: The question "which desires are truly mine, and which are imposed upon me?" is more urgent than ever in an age of advertising, social media, and constant stimulation. Epicurus forces you to ask what you really need—and the answer is surprisingly little.
epicurus-en-het-verlangen-naar-rust.openVragen:
• Is the distinction between natural and unnatural desires sustainable? Who determines what is "natural"?
  • How does Epicurus' atomism relate to modern particle physics? He took it over from Democritus, but added the clinamen—a random swerve of atoms that makes free will possible. Is that a forerunner of quantum indeterminism?
epicurus-en-het-verlangen-naar-rust.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 27: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Epicurus, Letter to Menoeceus (freely available online)

faseovergangen-wanneer-de-natuur-plotsel.titel: Phase Transitions — When Nature Suddenly Changes Its Mind
faseovergangen-wanneer-de-natuur-plotsel.kern: In a phase transition, matter abruptly changes character: water becomes ice, an iron rod becomes magnetic, the Higgs field freezes and gives particles mass. The mathematics underlying all these events is structurally identical — an ice cube in your freezer and the Higgs mechanism are the same phenomenon at radically different scales.
faseovergangen-wanneer-de-natuur-plotsel.uitleg:
The most familiar phase transition is water freezing. At 0°C, liquid water — molecules moving freely, no preferred direction, high symmetry — suddenly becomes ice: molecules locked in a crystal lattice with specific orientations and structure. The transition is sharp: at 0.1°C you have water, at -0.1°C you have ice. No gradual intermediate state.

A magnet displays the same pattern. Above the Curie point (770°C for iron), atomic magnets point randomly in all directions. There is no preferred direction — high symmetry. The material is not magnetic. Cool it below the Curie point and suddenly the magnets align in one direction. Symmetry breaks: there is now a north and a south. Magnetism appears, abruptly, like a switch being flipped.

What happens at this transition with entropy and symmetry is precisely the pattern that runs through the entire vault:

Symmetry decreases. Above the Curie point, every direction is equivalent. Below it, it is not — there is a chosen direction. The high symmetry of the disordered state is broken.

Local entropy decreases. The spin configurations of the magnets go from random (many possible configurations, high entropy) to ordered (few configurations, low entropy). The ordering of the magnets is a decrease in entropy — locally.

But global entropy increases. The rod releases heat to the surroundings as it cools. That heat increases the entropy of the surroundings more than the ordering of the spins costs. The second law of thermodynamics is not violated — the accounting works out, but you must count the entire system, not just the rod.

This is exactly the same principle as in star formation, in photosynthesis, in composting, in every process where local structure appears: order here, more disorder there. Nature always pays.

The astounding insight is that the mathematics describing how water freezes — Landau's theory of phase transitions — is structurally identical to the mathematics describing how the Higgs field freezes in the early universe. In both cases:

  1. A system has a symmetric state at high temperature (all directions equal, all particles massless)
  2. The system cools and reaches a critical threshold
  3. Symmetry breaks suddenly — the system "chooses" a direction
  4. A new property appears that wasn't there before (magnetism, mass)

The difference lies in scale and consequences — with a magnet you get a refrigerator magnet, with the Higgs field you get the mass of all matter in the universe — but the mechanism is the same.

Carroll emphasizes that this is not a metaphor. The mathematical structure is literally identical. The theory of phase transitions and symmetry breaking, developed in the 1930s–1960s by Landau, Ginzburg, and Wilson, turned out to be the key to particle physics. The Standard Model is essentially a theory of phase transitions in the early universe.

The entire history of the universe can be read as a series of phase transitions: the universe cools step by step, and at each critical threshold a symmetry breaks, new structure appears, and the entropy of the whole increases. From quarks confining in protons, to atoms forming, to water freezing on a planet that only exists thanks to all those earlier transitions.
faseovergangen-wanneer-de-natuur-plotsel.waarom:
Phase transitions make the abstract concrete. When someone asks "what is symmetry breaking?", you can pull an ice cube from the freezer. The symmetry of liquid water (every direction equal) is broken in the ordered structure of the crystal — and in precisely the same way, the symmetry of the early universe was broken in the structure of the matter we know. It is the same phenomenon, from your kitchen counter to the Big Bang.

It is also the moment when Aristotle's potentiality to actuality becomes most tangible: at the Curie point, both states (magnetic and non-magnetic) exist as possibility. Temperature determines which is realized. The transition is abrupt — a springpoint when nature "chooses."
faseovergangen-wanneer-de-natuur-plotsel.openVragen:
• Are there phase transitions in biology? The moment a cell divides, or a population suddenly collapses — are those analogous phenomena?
  • The universe has undergone multiple phase transitions as it cooled. Are there more to be expected, or have all the symmetries that can break already broken?
  • Phase transitions exhibit "critical phenomena" — fluctuations at all scales, self-similarity. Is that related to fractal patterns in nature?
faseovergangen-wanneer-de-natuur-plotsel.verder_lezen:

  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #17 (Matter) — how the Higgs field "freezes": https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 1 (Atoms in Motion) — phase transitions of matter at atomic scales: https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_01.html
  • Al-Khalili, Atom (BBC Four, 2007, part 3) — the structure of matter and the role of symmetry

fenologie.kern: Phenology is the study of timing in biology: when does the hawthorn bloom, when does the great tit arrive, when do the swifts return? These are the annual appointments of an ecosystem — appointments that must be kept for things to add up.
fenologie.uitleg:
The word comes from Greek: phaino (to appear) and logos (study). The name is nineteenth-century, but the practice is much older. Japanese court officials carefully recorded from the ninth century onwards when cherry blossoms bloomed in Kyoto — an unbroken series of 1,200 years of bloom data, one of the longest climate records we have. In England, Robert Marsham from 1736 onwards recorded when the first blackbird sang and oak buds opened. His family continued it for 200 years. These are the quiet datasets from which we can now read climate change.

The modern urgency of phenology lies in that shift. Climate change shifts the appointments — but not all by the same amount. Some species respond to temperature (plants, insects), others to day length (migratory birds). If the two drift apart, phenological mismatch occurs: the appointment is missed.

The classic example is the great tit in an oak forest. Oak leaves emerge earlier due to warmer springs; the winter moth caterpillars that live on those young leaves also emerge earlier; but great tit pairs lay their eggs based on day length, which does not shift. When the chicks hatch after the caterpillar peak, there is less food. Reduced breeding success. It is not the species that respond poorly to warmth that succumb first — it is the species whose partners do shift.

Dave Goulson describes in The Garden Jungle how this is now becoming visible in Dutch and British gardens. Early pollinators appear before their plants bloom. Some moths emerge so early that their caterpillars find no food. The ecosystem is out of phase, and it does not recover on its own — not within a human lifetime.

In your own garden, phenology is the most accessible field in biology. A notebook and a calendar are enough. Record: first snowdrop, first bumblebee sighting (which species?), first blackbird song before sunrise, first swallow, the date when hedge mustard is in full bloom. Three years of data and you have your own phenological almanac; ten years and you see trends that no one else in your garden sees. In the Netherlands, Nature's Calendar collects observations from volunteers — already for more than twenty years.
fenologie.waarom: Phenology transforms climate change from an abstract graph into a personal observation. Not "average temperature has risen," but "the first bumblebee now arrives three weeks earlier than ten years ago." And it makes visible that the problem is not warmth alone, but coordination — an ecosystem that must suddenly revise centuries-old appointments without time to do so neatly. For the attentive observer, a garden is a phenological laboratory that issues a report every year.
fenologie.openVragen:
• How long does it take for species to resynchronize their phenology with one another — generations, centuries, or not at all?
  • Which phenological shifts are reversible if climate change is stopped, and which are not?
fenologie.verder_lezen:
• Nature's Calendar: naturetoday.com — submit and view observations
  • Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — multiple passages on shifting insect phenology
  • UK Phenology Network: naturescalendar.woodlandtrust.org.uk — British equivalent with clear visualizations
  • Marsham Phenological Database (1736–): one of the longest personal series in the world, described on the British Royal Meteorological Society website

het-mes-van-ockham.kern: Entia non sunt multiplicanda praeter necessitatem — entities must not be multiplied without necessity. When two explanations account for the same phenomenon equally well, choose the simplest: the one with the fewest assumptions, the fewest invisible entities, the smallest theoretical weight.
het-mes-van-ockham.uitleg:
The razor is named after William of Ockham (ca. 1287–1347), an English Franciscan monk and logician. He used the principle sharply and consistently — hence its naming — but did not invent it. Aristotle and earlier scholastics formulated similar rules. Ockham's merit was honing it into a razor.

Important to understand: the razor is a methodological prescription, not a metaphysical claim. It does not say "reality is simple" — it says "begin with the simple hypothesis and add complexity only if the facts compel it". Russell valued this in Ockham: a sober, logical brake on the baroque entity-production of scholasticism (angels, forms, essences, qualities — an entire taxonomic forest of invisible beings, all "explaining" on no other evidence than that they were conceptually convenient).

In modern science the razor is everywhere. Why we do not assume the earth is flat: not because flatness is impossible, but because a flat earth requires a whole cascade of additional assumptions (light-refraction curves, conspiracies, physics that works differently at the horizon) to explain observations. A round earth explains the same with less. The same holds for evolution versus special creation, for heliocentrism versus epicycles, for relativity versus the aether.

But the razor is not a truth detector. Dark matter and dark energy are not obviously parsimonious postulates — invisible entities making up 95% of the universe — and yet they are the simplest explanation within the general-relativistic framework for what we actually observe (rotation curves, supernova data, the cosmic microwave background). "Simple" is always relative to a framework. What is unnecessary multiplication in one paradigm can be the necessary minimum assumption in another.

In combination with Hume's problem, the razor takes on an interesting role. If we cannot prove which hypothesis is correct, we can at least be economical. Parsimony is not a truth claim, but a pragmatic rule of thumb where provable certainty fails.
het-mes-van-ockham.waarom: The razor teaches us that an explanation that "fits everything" often fits too much — just like a conspiracy theory that treats every counterargument as part of the conspiracy. True explanatory power lies not in flexibility but in constraint. A good theory rules things out. The razor is therefore not only a scientific tool but also an everyday thinking tool: whenever you hear an explanation, ask whether there is not a simpler one that accounts for the same thing.
het-mes-van-ockham.openVragen:
• How do you measure "simplicity" objectively? Number of assumptions, or something else?
  • Is the razor ultimately an aesthetic principle, a pragmatic principle, or something with metaphysical weight?
het-mes-van-ockham.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book II Part II Ch. XIV "Franciscan Schoolmen" — section on Ockham, PDF p. 471
  • Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, "William of Ockham" — authoritative open-source source
  • Sober, Ockham's Razors: A User's Manual (2015) — modern book on how the principle actually functions in science

het-roodverschoven-foton-waar-energie-ve.titel: The Redshifted Photon — Where Energy Disappears
het-roodverschoven-foton-waar-energie-ve.kern: A photon traveling through the expanding universe loses energy — its wavelength stretches with space itself. That energy goes nowhere. It is simply gone. This is not a bookkeeping error but proof that energy conservation is not an absolute law, precisely as Noether's theorem predicts.
het-roodverschoven-foton-waar-energie-ve.uitleg:
Imagine the purest experiment conceivable. One photon, alone in a vacuum, no interactions, no collisions. The photon travels through the universe. Space expands. The photon's wavelength stretches with it — growing longer, shifting from blue to red, from visible light to infrared, eventually to microwaves. Longer wavelength means lower energy. The photon loses energy, measurably and irreversibly.

The question your intuition immediately raises: where has that energy gone? There is no particle that captures the energy. There is no field that grows warmer. There is no reservoir. The energy vanishes, and there is no violation of natural law — because the law that guarantees energy conservation does not apply here.

Noether's theorem proves that energy conservation follows from time symmetry: the assumption that the laws of physics tomorrow work the same as today. In a laboratory, on a planet, even in a galaxy, that symmetry is excellent intact. But on cosmological scales, where space itself expands, the background on which the laws play is no longer static. Spacetime changes. Time symmetry is broken. And therefore energy conservation does not strictly hold.

Carroll formulates it sharply: it is not that the photon "loses energy to space." Space is not a thing that can receive energy. It is that the concept "the total energy of the universe" in Einstein's general relativity is not a well-defined quantity. The bookkeeping that works flawlessly in a laboratory does not apply on cosmic scales — not because we do not measure well enough, but because the mathematical structure of the theory forbids it.

This feels deeply unsatisfying. In every experiment you can perform on Earth, energy is conserved. No exceptions. That principle is so fundamental that it feels like an absolute truth. But it is not an absolute truth — it is a consequence of a symmetry, and symmetries can break.

The reverse side makes it even stranger. Dark energy — the mysterious force behind accelerated expansion — has a constant energy density per volume. But the volume of the universe grows. That means the total dark energy increases. Where does it come from? Nowhere. For the same reason: the symmetry that guarantees energy conservation is broken.

Photons lose energy through expansion. Dark energy gains energy through expansion. They are two sides of the same coin — both are consequences of an expanding spacetime in which Noether's time symmetry does not hold.

The cosmic microwave background is the most dramatic example. The photons emitted 380,000 years after the Big Bang had a temperature of ±3000 K — glowing orange, visible light. Now, 13.8 billion years later, they have stretched into microwaves with a temperature of 2.7 K. The same photons, but with a thousand times less energy. That energy has gone nowhere. It has vanished into the growing space.
het-roodverschoven-foton-waar-energie-ve.waarom:
This is the concept that closes and tests the entire chain we have built in the vault. Noether's theorem says: symmetry → conservation law. Symmetry breaking shows that broken symmetries create structure. The synthesis note describes the universe as a cascade of symmetry breaking. And this photon example shows the ultimate consequence: even energy conservation — the most trusted law in all of physics — is not absolute but dependent on a symmetry that is broken on cosmic scales.

That is not a disappointment but a liberation. It shows that physics does not consist of unchangeable dogmas but of principles that depend on context. At your scale, energy conservation works flawlessly. At the scale of the universe, it does not. Both are true, and Noether's theorem explains why.
het-roodverschoven-foton-waar-energie-ve.openVragen:
• If energy conservation does not hold on cosmic scales, are there other conservation laws that are also broken by expansion?
  • The cosmic microwave background has been losing energy for 13.8 billion years. Is there a point at which the photons have so little energy that they effectively "disappear"?
  • If dark energy increases while photons lose energy, is the universe heading toward a state consisting only of dark energy?

  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #1 (Conservation) — Noether and the limits of conservation laws: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Carroll, blog post "Energy Is Not Conserved": https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/blog/2010/02/22/energy-is-not-conserved/
