  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 5 (The Theory of Gravitation): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_07.html

het-seizoensritme-van-insecten-in-je-tui.titel: The Seasonal Rhythm of Garden Insects
het-seizoensritme-van-insecten-in-je-tui.kern: Insects do not appear randomly throughout the year but follow a predictable seasonal pattern directly linked to temperature, day length, and flowering — and understanding that pattern changes how you garden.
het-seizoensritme-van-insecten-in-je-tui.uitleg:
The insect year begins earlier than most people think. As early as February, at the first rays of sun on a sheltered wall, overwintering females of the garden bumblebee and the peacock butterfly appear. They urgently need nectar after months without food — hence the importance of early spring bloomers.

March-April is startup time. Solitary bees (mason bees, mining bees) are now most active. They nest in hollow stems, wall crevices, and bare soil. Unlike honeybees, they live alone — each female builds her own nest and lays her own eggs. They are particularly effective pollinators of fruit trees, better than honeybees because they are messier: they scatter more pollen.

May-June is the peak. Insect life explodes: ladybugs hunt aphids, hoverflies pollinate and their larvae eat aphids, butterflies appear on butterfly bushes and thistles. This is when the entire food chain turns — insectivorous birds (blue tit, spotted flycatcher) now feed their young with thousands of caterpillars per day.

July-August the picture shifts. Many spring species are past, but nocturnal moths, grasshoppers, and dragonflies increase. Wild bee activity shifts toward late bloomers: ivy, heather, late-flowering composites.

September-October is the wind-down. The last generation of butterflies (red admiral, peacock butterfly) fuel up on overripe fruit and ivy flowers. Bumblebee queens search for overwintering spots. Most insects die, but their eggs, pupae, or overwintering adults await the next spring.

What this means for garden management: continuity of flowering is more important than peaks. A garden that only explodes in June starves insects in March and October. The ideal is continuous blooming from February to November, which aligns perfectly with the concept of succession: different plant layers that follow each other in flowering.
het-seizoensritme-van-insecten-in-je-tui.waarom: Insects are the invisible engine of your garden. They pollinate your fruit trees, control pest insects, feed the birds, and process organic matter. Understanding the seasonal rhythm transforms "insects" from something that happens to you into something you can facilitate.
het-seizoensritme-van-insecten-in-je-tui.openVragen:
• Which late bloomers (September-November) are most valuable for overwintering insects in the Gooi region?
  • How significant is the impact of an insect hotel compared to simply leaving dead stems standing?
het-seizoensritme-van-insecten-in-je-tui.verder_lezen:
• Dave Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — seasonal ecology of garden insects
  • Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 2 (One Voice in the Cosmic Fugue) — evolution and the richness of life forms: https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf

humes-probleem-van-inductie.kern: We assume the sun will rise tomorrow because it always has until now. But that step — from "so far" to "always" — is logically unprovable. Hume showed that our entire edifice of empirical knowledge rests on a habit of thought, not on reason.
humes-probleem-van-inductie.uitleg:
David Hume published A Treatise of Human Nature in 1739, at the age of twenty-eight. The book, by his own account, "fell dead-born from the press" — and only decades later did it become clear what he had actually said. He had pulled away the foundation of science from beneath science itself.

The argument is simple and all the more uncomfortable for it. Induction is the step from observed patterns to general laws. We see water boil repeatedly at 100°C, and conclude that it always does so. But that conclusion is not deductively valid. There is no logical contradiction in a world where water tomorrow suddenly boils at 80°C. Hume then showed that we cannot justify the validity of induction empirically either — because any attempt uses induction itself ("induction has worked well so far, so it will continue to work"). That is circular.

What is left? According to Hume: custom. We expect the future to resemble the past because our mind is built that way, not because we can prove it. That applies to physics, to medicine, and to the simple fact that you expect your legs to carry you tomorrow morning.

Russell considered this one of the most important problems in philosophy and took it seriously his entire life. In The Problems of Philosophy (1912) he argues that we must accept the principle of induction as an axiom — not provable, but indispensable for science. Not a comfortable position, but an honest one.

In the twentieth century, Karl Popper chose a different route: not verification but falsification. Theories are not confirmed by instances, but tested through attempts at refutation. That shifts the problem — falsification still presupposes that today's laws hold tomorrow. The problem has never been solved, only circumvented.
humes-probleem-van-inductie.waarom: The uncomfortable consequence is that our entire scientific enterprise — nuclear physics, medicine, climate models — ultimately rests on something unprovable. That does not invalidate science, but it does make it more fragile than many people think. Hume's point is not skepticism meant to combat science, but an attitude of honesty about what exactly we are doing. We trust induction without foundation, and that is the best we have for now.
humes-probleem-van-inductie.openVragen:
• Is Russell's solution — accepting induction as an axiom — a real solution or a disguised capitulation?
  • How does the problem of induction relate to modern machine learning, which is in essence automated induction?
humes-probleem-van-inductie.verder_lezen:
• Russell, The Problems of Philosophy (1912), Ch. VI "On Induction" — free on Project Gutenberg
  • Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book III Part I Ch. XVII "Hume" — PDF p. 632
  • Hume, An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (1748), Section IV — concise version of the argument, Gutenberg link

inheemse-versus-exotische-planten.kern: A native hawthorn hosts more than 200 insect species. An exotic conifer almost none. The difference explains why plant choice is the most determining factor for biodiversity in your garden.
inheemse-versus-exotische-planten.uitleg:
Native plants — species that naturally occur here and have evolved together with local insect life for thousands of years — form the foundation of the food web. Caterpillars of most butterfly species can only survive on specific native host plants. Brimstone butterfly eggs are laid exclusively on alder buckthorn and common buckthorn. Orange tip caterpillars eat only cuckoo flower and garlic mustard. No native host plant = no caterpillar = no butterfly = less food for insectivorous birds.

Exotic plants — rhododendron, cherry laurel, bamboo, many popular garden centre plants — are largely food deserts for local insect life. They look green but function ecologically like plastic. A hedge of cherry laurel is for biodiversity the equivalent of a fence.

That doesn't mean every exotic is bad. Some garden plants from Southern Europe or Asia do provide nectar to generalist pollinators (lavender, Verbena bonariensis). But they lack the deep coevolutionary relationships that native plants have: the specific caterpillars, the solitary bees perfectly attuned to that flower, the fungi that cooperate with those roots.

Practical rule of thumb: aim for a minimum of 70% native planting as a foundation, supplemented with selected exotics that add nectar value during periods when native species flower little (especially late summer and autumn).

Strong native choices for a garden in the Gooi: hawthorn (shrub/tree, 200+ insect species), blackthorn (early flowering, fruit for birds), wild privet (pollinators, berries), ivy (essential autumn bloomer for bumblebees and butterflies), wild honeysuckle (night butterflies), pollard willow (early pollen source).
inheemse-versus-exotische-planten.waarom: Plant choice is the most impactful decision you can make in your garden. It's not the nest boxes, not the insect hotel, not the pond — it's what's in the ground. One hawthorn does more ecologically than ten insect hotels.
inheemse-versus-exotische-planten.openVragen:
• Does climate change shift the definition of "native"? Species now found in Belgium but not the Netherlands are moving northward.
  • Is a garden with 100% native plants realistic in an urban environment, or is a mixed form the best aesthetic and ecological compromise?
inheemse-versus-exotische-planten.verder_lezen:
• Dave Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — the difference between native and exotic in garden context
  • Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 2 (One Voice in the Cosmic Fugue) — coevolution as a fundamental biological principle: https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf

keplers-wetten.kern: In the early 17th century, Johannes Kepler discovered that planets do not move in perfect circles around the sun, but in ellipses — and he formulated three laws that describe their motion with precision.
keplers-wetten.uitleg:
For two thousand years, the idea dominated that celestial motions had to be circular. The circle was the "perfect" form — Plato and Aristotle demanded it, Pythagoras would have expected it, and Copernicus still adhered to it when he placed the sun at the center. But the observations never quite matched. Endless corrections were needed (epicycles — circles upon circles) to make the data fit.

Kepler, working with the exceptionally accurate observations of Tycho Brahe, did what no one before him had dared: he let go of the circle. After years of calculation, he discovered that Mars's orbit is an ellipse with the sun at one focus. That became his first law.

His second law describes how fast a planet moves: closer to the sun it goes faster, farther away it goes slower, in such a way that the line from sun to planet sweeps out equal areas in equal times.

His third law connects orbital period to distance: the square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the average distance from the sun. Jupiter, five times farther than Earth, takes not five but nearly twelve years to orbit.

Sagan describes in Cosmos how painful this process was for Kepler. He himself was a mystic who believed in the harmony of the spheres — he wanted the planetary orbits to fit into a schema of nested Platonic solids. But the data forced him to abandon his own conviction. That is science at its strongest: the willingness to let go of a beautiful idea when the facts do not support it.

Feynman cites Kepler's laws as the empirical foundation upon which Newton built his theory of gravitation. Newton showed that all three of Kepler's laws automatically follow from a single simple assumption: gravitational force decreases with the square of the distance.
keplers-wetten.waarom: It is the moment when aesthetics lost to data. Two thousand years of circular dogma was broken by someone who had the courage to take measurements more seriously than tradition. That makes Kepler not only an astronomer but a hero of the scientific method.
keplers-wetten.openVragen:
• Why were ellipses so difficult to accept? Is there a psychological explanation for the human preference for symmetrical forms?
  • Kepler's laws describe what happens, but not why. That "why" came only with Newton. Is there a modern parallel — phenomena we describe but do not explain?
keplers-wetten.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 3 (The Harmony of Worlds): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 5 (The Theory of Gravitation): https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_07.html

kringlopen-en-energiestromen-waarom-de-b.titel: Cycles and Energy Flows — why the banana returns but the heat does not
kringlopen-en-energiestromen-waarom-de-b.kern: In nature, matter circulates — the same carbon atoms move from air to leaf to fruit to compost to soil to root and back to leaf. But energy flows, in one direction, from the sun into space. Cycles exist only because that one-way flow drives them.
kringlopen-en-energiestromen-waarom-de-b.uitleg:
Take the cycle of a banana. The banana tree captures sunlight through photosynthesis and uses it to convert CO₂ from the air and water from the ground into sugars — the flesh of the banana. You eat the banana, throw the peel on a bokashi or compost heap. Microorganisms and earthworms break down the peel into humus and minerals. Those nutrients return to the tree's roots via the soil food web and mycorrhizal networks. The tree builds a new banana from them. The cycle is complete.

But there is a difference between what circulates and what does not. The matter cycles: the carbon atoms in the new banana are literally the same atoms that once were in the old peel, and before that in the air, and before that perhaps in a dinosaur. Atoms are indestructible (at least on biological timescales) and are recycled endlessly.

The energy does not cycle. At every step in the cycle, some energy is lost as heat — infrared radiation that the earth radiates into cold space. The tree captures high-energy sun photons and converts them into chemical energy (sugars). Your body burns those sugars and gives off heat. The compost heap breaks down the peel and gives off heat. At every step, useful energy is converted into useless heat. That is entropy increase: not a loss of energy (the total amount is conserved) but a loss of usefulness.

The cycle is therefore not a perpetual motion machine. It is a water wheel. The water (matter) goes round and round, but the river (energy from the sun) drives the wheel. Stop the river — the sun dies — and the wheel stands still. Within one generation of trees, photosynthesis stops, the compost becomes exhausted, the cycle breaks apart.

This is the difference between the first and second laws of thermodynamics in action. The first law says: energy is conserved, nothing is lost. The second law says: with every conversion, energy becomes slightly less useful. The cycle of matter obeys the first law. The one-way flow of energy obeys the second.

The sun itself is not an eternal source either. It burns hydrogen into helium through nuclear fusion — a process that stops in approximately 5 billion years when the fuel runs out. The river that drives all cycles on Earth is finite. And the energy the sun radiates is ultimately stretched into ever longer wavelengths by the expansion of the universe, until it is so thinly dispersed that nothing can be done with it.

Follow the line all the way through and you see the complete cascade: the Big Bang delivered the low-entropy initial state. Gravity formed stars. Stars produce the energy flow that drives cycles on planets. Those cycles create local order — trees, bananas, compost heaps, garden ecosystems, you — at the cost of global entropy increase. And ultimately, on a timescale that defies imagination, the universe reaches maximum entropy and all cycles, all flows, all processes stop.

But that is trillions of years away. Today the sun shines, the banana grows, and the cycle turns. And every time you throw a banana peel on the compost heap, you participate in a process as old as life itself — recycling matter while the sun pays the energy bill.
kringlopen-en-energiestromen-waarom-de-b.waarom:
It changes how you look at your garden. A composter who sees only cycles thinks the system sustains itself. A composter who also sees the energy flow understands that everything — from the compost heap to the rainforest to the entire biosphere — depends on one source: the sun. And that source is finite, not on human timescales, but on cosmic timescales.

It also connects the three domains of the vault in the most direct way. Philosophy asks: where does everything come from (arche)? Cosmology answers: from a low-entropy initial state, driven by gravity and nuclear fusion. Nature shows: and this is what that looks like on the scale of a backyard — a banana peel that returns as a banana, driven by starlight.
kringlopen-en-energiestromen-waarom-de-b.openVragen:
• If all energy on Earth ultimately comes from the sun, how many "sunlight steps" are there between a photon and the banana on your plate?
  • Earth heat (geothermal energy) is an exception — it comes from radioactive decay in the Earth's core, not from the sun. How large is the contribution of Earth heat to biological cycles compared to sunlight?
  • Is it worthwhile to design a garden as an as-efficient-as-possible "solar energy catcher" — maximum photosynthesis per square meter?
kringlopen-en-energiestromen-waarom-de-b.verder_lezen:
• Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 2 (One Voice in the Cosmic Fugue) — life as a cosmic phenomenon driven by starlight: https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #19 (Entropy) — the sun as a low-entropy energy source: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 4 (Conservation of Energy) — energy conservation and the chain of conversions: https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_04.html
  • Sheldrake, Entangled Life (2020) — fungi as the recyclers of the cycle: https://archive.org/details/entangled-life_202203

nachtvlinders-als-vergeten-bestuivers.kern: Nocturnal moths pollinate more plant species than day-flying butterflies and bees combined, yet are almost never mentioned in conversations about pollinators. They work the night shift, invisible and underestimated.
nachtvlinders-als-vergeten-bestuivers.uitleg:
There are approximately 2,500 species of nocturnal moths in the Netherlands compared to only 55 species of day-flying butterflies. That ratio — 45:1 — already indicates how much diversity we overlook. Recent studies have shown that nocturnal moths visit a broader spectrum of plant species than day-active pollinators and transport pollen over greater distances.

Nocturnal moths pollinate plants specifically adapted to them: white or light-colored flowers that open in the evening and smell strongly (honeysuckle, night-flowering catchfly, evening primrose, willowherb). That scent is their equivalent of the bright colors that attract day-flying butterflies — nocturnal moths navigate by smell, not by sight.

The problem: light pollution. Street lighting, garden spotlights and illuminated windows draw nocturnal moths away from flowers and toward artificial light, where they circle exhausted until they die or are eaten. Research shows that areas with more artificial light have measurably less pollination by nocturnal moths.

What you can do: plant night-blooming species (wild honeysuckle, night-flowering catchfly), avoid garden lighting or use warm-white LED with a timer, and leave a messy corner with grass tufts — many nocturnal moth caterpillars hide during the day in grass bases.
nachtvlinders-als-vergeten-bestuivers.waarom: We optimize gardens for day-active pollinators and forget that the night has a complete second pollination shift. Reducing light pollution in your garden is one of the simplest ways to promote biodiversity — it literally costs nothing, you just have to turn off a lamp.
nachtvlinders-als-vergeten-bestuivers.openVragen:
• Which nocturnal moth species are most common in gardens in the Gooi region?
  • What is the effect of a single street lamp on pollination within a 50-meter radius?
nachtvlinders-als-vergeten-bestuivers.verder_lezen:
• Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — chapter on nocturnal moths
  • Sheldrake, Entangled Life (2020) — the plants pollinated by nocturnal moths are often mycorrhiza-dependent

olbers-paradox-waarom-is-de-nacht-donker.titel: Olbers' Paradox — Why is the Night Dark?
olbers-paradox-waarom-is-de-nacht-donker.kern: If the universe is infinitely large and contains stars everywhere, then every line of sight across the sky should eventually end on a star. The night sky should therefore not be dark but blindingly bright. That it is not tells us something profound about the nature of the universe.
olbers-paradox-waarom-is-de-nacht-donker.uitleg:
The paradox is older than Olbers (who reformulated it in 1823) — Kepler and Halley struggled with it already. The reasoning is simple: if you stand in an infinite forest, you will eventually see a tree in every direction, no matter how far away. In the same way, in an infinite universe full of stars you should see a stellar surface in every direction. The entire sky should glow like the surface of the sun.

Several solutions have been proposed — dust that absorbs light, stars that are too faint — but they don't work. Dust that absorbs light becomes hot itself and begins to radiate. And the paradox is not about individual stars but about the total amount of light.

The real solution combines two cosmological facts. First: the universe is not infinitely old. It is approximately 13.8 billion years old (Big Bang), which means that light from stars further away than 13.8 billion light-years has not yet reached us. There is a horizon. Second: Redshift shifts the light from distant stars toward longer wavelengths, eventually far beyond the visible spectrum. Distant stars are not only far away but have also become invisible.

The dark night sky is therefore not a triviality but cosmological proof: the universe had a beginning, and it is expanding. If either of these two things were not true, the night would not be dark.

Al-Khalili addresses this in Paradox as one of nine "greatest enigmas" and emphasizes that it is a paradox that has been solved — but that the solution is more surprising than the problem.
olbers-paradox-waarom-is-de-nacht-donker.waarom: It is one of the most beautiful examples of how an everyday observation (the night is dark) has a profound cosmological implication. You don't need to look at the stars to do cosmology — you only need to ask why you don't see them everywhere.
olbers-paradox-waarom-is-de-nacht-donker.openVragen:
• If the universe continues to expand forever, does the night become progressively darker as more and more galaxies disappear beyond our horizon?
  • Is there an analogous argument for gravity — a "gravitational Olbers' paradox"?
olbers-paradox-waarom-is-de-nacht-donker.verder_lezen:
• Al-Khalili, Paradox: The Nine Greatest Enigmas in Physics (2012) — Olbers' paradox as opening chapter
  • Sagan, Cosmos, Ch. 10 (The Edge of Forever): https://www.arvindguptatoys.com/arvindgupta/sagancosmos.pdf

olbers-paradox.kern: Why is the night sky dark? If the universe is infinitely large, infinitely old, and more or less uniformly filled with stars, it should not be. Something in those premises is wrong — and that something tells us how the universe is actually constructed.
olbers-paradox.uitleg:
Heinrich Wilhelm Olbers, a German physician and astronomer, formulated the problem in 1823. He was not the first — Kepler, Halley, and Cheseaux had made similar observations — but Olbers' version endured, hence the name.

The reasoning is beautifully simple. Imagine you are standing in an infinitely dense forest. In whichever direction you look, your eye eventually encounters a tree trunk. Replace the trees with stars and you have Olbers' argument: in an infinite universe full of stars, every line of sight from Earth eventually ends on the surface of a star. The night sky should therefore be as bright as the surface of the sun. It is not. The question is: why not?

The solution lies in two escape routes, and both are necessary.

The first is that the universe is not infinitely old. It is approximately 13.8 billion years old. Light travels at finite speed. From stars further than 13.8 billion light-years away, light simply has not reached us. We look not only through space, we look through time — and time has run out. The Big Bang sets a limit on what we can ever see, and that limit spares our nights.

The second is expansion. The universe expands, so light from distant objects is redshifted and in extreme cases shifted entirely out of the visible spectrum. What remains of the glow of the early universe we find as the cosmic microwave background — a form of the "bright sky" that Olbers predicted, only shifted to microwaves we cannot see with the naked eye. With a radio telescope at the right frequency, the entire sky does glow. Olbers was right; only the senses with which we look have the wrong sensitivity.

A curious detail: Edgar Allan Poe, not a scientist but a poet, gave in his essay Eureka (1848) intuitively the correct solution — without the mathematics, only with poetic reasoning. The history of science moves us occasionally.
olbers-paradox.waarom: Olbers' paradox is one of the most beautiful examples of armchair philosophy predicting physics. With logic alone and the assumption that light travels at finite speed, you can reason that a static infinite universe is impossible. That is a different path to the same conclusion as Hubble's expansion and Einstein's field equations. Three completely different trails all lead to the Big Bang model — which statistically speaking is a strong sign that the model is correct.
olbers-paradox.openVragen:
• If the universe continues to expand forever, does the night become truly darker in the very distant future as galaxies disappear beyond the horizon?
  • Is there an analogous "gravitational Olbers' paradox" — why are we not crushed by the gravitational pull of an infinite universe?
olbers-paradox.verder_lezen:
• Carroll, The Big Picture (2016), chapter on cosmology
  • Wikipedia, "Olbers' paradox" — clear mathematical exposition
  • Poe, Eureka: A Prose Poem (1848) — freely available on Project Gutenberg, a curiosity with fragments of insight

platos-ideeenleer.kern: Behind the changeable world we perceive, according to Plato, exists a timeless reality of perfect "Forms" or "Ideas" — and only those Forms are truly real.
platos-ideeenleer.uitleg:
Imagine a circle you draw on paper. It is never perfect — there are irregularities in it, the line has thickness, the shape is an approximation. Yet you know what a perfect circle is, even though you have never seen one. Plato concludes: there must exist a perfect Circle somewhere, not in the physical world but in a higher reality. That is the Form of the circle. The same applies to Beauty, Justice, Equality — everything we recognize in the world as an imperfect version of something ideal.

For Plato, the physical world is a shadow world: a reflection of the true world of Forms. His famous allegory of the cave illustrates this — people are chained in a cave and see only shadows on a wall. They think the shadows are reality, until someone breaks free, goes outside, and sees the actual sunlight.

Russell sees in Plato the heir of two Presocratics at once. From Parmenides he takes the idea that true reality is unchanging. From Pythagoras he takes the idea that mathematical abstractions are more real than perceivable things. The theory of forms is in a sense the synthesis of both.

Yet Russell is also critical: he finds that Plato too readily assumes that every word (justice, beauty) must have a corresponding Form. And he warns that the theory of forms can become politically dangerous when philosophers claim to have access to the "true" reality that ordinary people cannot see — a claim Plato himself makes in The Republic.

Nevertheless, the influence is enormous. All of Western philosophy is, as Whitehead famously remarked, "a series of footnotes to Plato." And in physics there lives a Platonic echo: are the mathematical laws of nature discoveries (Plato) or inventions (anti-Plato)? That debate continues still.
platos-ideeenleer.waarom: The theory of forms forces you to take a stance on the nature of abstract objects. Do numbers, laws, mathematical structures exist "really" somewhere — or are they useful fictions? This is not an academic question: it touches on how we understand science. If the laws of quantum mechanics are "real" in a Platonic sense, then physics describes something beyond our perception. If they are not, then physics is merely an effective model.
platos-ideeenleer.openVragen:
• Is the Standard Model of particle physics a Platonic Form that we discover, or a human construction that happens to work?
  • Has Plato's distinction between appearance and reality influenced the way we interpret quantum mechanics (the observable world as "unreal")?
platos-ideeenleer.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 15: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #24 (Science) — the question of whether mathematical laws are discovered or invented: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/

regenwormen-als-bodembouwers.kern: Darwin devoted his final major work not to evolution but to earthworms. He calculated that in England they process the complete upper layer of soil through their bodies annually. They are the quietest and most underestimated architects of every garden ecosystem.
regenwormen-als-bodembouwers.uitleg:
Charles Darwin published in 1881 The Formation of Vegetable Mould through the Action of Worms — his bestselling book during his lifetime. His conclusion: earthworms are the most important factor in forming and maintaining fertile soil. It was a revolutionary idea in his time, and it remains valid today.

A healthy Dutch garden soil contains 100-400 earthworms per square meter. They perform three things that no other organism can do as effectively.

First: mixing. Worms pull organic material (leaves, dead roots) downward and bring mineral soil upward. This prevents stratification and creates a homogeneous, fertile topsoil. This is exactly what digging also does, but worms do it continuously, without destroying the structure.

Second: aeration. Worm tunnels create a network of channels through which water infiltrates and roots can grow deeper. A soil with abundant worm activity absorbs rain much faster than compacted soil — relevant given increasing cloudbursts from climate change.

Third: enrichment. Worm castings (worm humus) contain five times more nitrogen, seven times more phosphate, and eleven times more potassium than surrounding soil. Moreover, they are coated with mucus that feeds microorganisms — perfect starting points for mycorrhizal networks.

There are three ecological groups of earthworms that each play a different role. Litter-dwellers (red, small, living in the upper layer — the compost worm belongs here) process surface material. Topsoil-dwellers (pink, medium-sized) mix the upper 20 cm. Deep-burrowers (large, dark-headed — the common earthworm) create vertical tunnels more than a meter deep and pull leaves downward.

What kills worms: digging (destroys tunnels and worms physically), synthetic fertilizer (acidifies soil), pesticides (direct toxicity), and bare soil without organic material (no food). What promotes worms: mulching, composting, no-dig methods, and moist soil with abundant organic material.
regenwormen-als-bodembouwers.waarom: If Darwin was right — and 140 years of research confirms it — then the earthworm is the foundation of your garden. No worm, no healthy soil. No healthy soil, no healthy plant. It is the most direct chain in garden ecology, and the easiest to influence: stop digging, mulch, and the worms do the rest.
regenwormen-als-bodembouwers.openVragen:
• How many earthworms are there per square meter in my garden, and how do I measure that? (Mustard suspension method)
  • How quickly does a worm population recover after years of intensive digging?
regenwormen-als-bodembouwers.verder_lezen:
• Charles Darwin, The Formation of Vegetable Mould through the Action of Worms (1881, freely available online via Project Gutenberg)
  • Lowenfels & Lewis, Teaming with Microbes (2010) — worms in the context of the soil food web

speciale-relativiteit-niets-is-sneller-d.titel: Special Relativity — Nothing is Faster Than Light

stoicisme-wat-je-wel-en-niet-kunt-beheer.titel: Stoicism — What You Can and Cannot Control
stoicisme-wat-je-wel-en-niet-kunt-beheer.kern: The Stoics held that happiness does not depend on what happens to you but on how you respond to it. The only thing truly within your power is your own judgment — everything else (health, possessions, reputation, even death) lies beyond your control and therefore deserves no emotional investment.
stoicisme-wat-je-wel-en-niet-kunt-beheer.uitleg:
Stoicism arose around 300 BCE in Athens, founded by Zeno of Citium (not to be confused with Zeno of Elea). It grew into one of the most influential philosophical schools of antiquity and counted among its followers both a slave (Epictetus) and an emperor (Marcus Aurelius).

The core idea is the dichotomy of control. Divide everything into two categories: things within your power (your judgments, your intentions, your responses) and things that are not (the weather, others' opinions, illness, death). Direct all your energy toward the first category and accept the second with equanimity — apatheia, not numbness but freedom from destructive emotions.

The Stoics saw the universe as one rational whole, governed by logos — a term they shared with Heraclitus. Everything that happens occurs according to cosmic reason. Your resistance to the course of events is like a dog tied to a moving cart: you can run along or be dragged. The outcome is the same; only your experience differs.

Russell recognizes the ethical power of Stoicism but criticizes the passivity that can follow from it. If everything that happens is "the will of fate," why should you fight injustice? The Stoics had an answer — you have the duty to act justly, but you must release yourself from the outcome — yet Russell finds this intellectually unsatisfying.

What makes Stoicism particularly relevant is that it was the first philosophy to be explicitly presented as an art of living: not a theory about the world but a daily practice. Epictetus's Enchiridion is essentially a manual for mental resilience — and the direct precursor to modern cognitive behavioral therapy.
stoicisme-wat-je-wel-en-niet-kunt-beheer.waarom: Of all ancient philosophies, Stoicism is the most directly applicable to daily life. The dichotomy of control is a thinking tool you can use in stress, setbacks, and situations you cannot change — precisely the situations that life as a chiropractor with three small children consists of.
stoicisme-wat-je-wel-en-niet-kunt-beheer.openVragen:
• Is Stoicism too passive? Where lies the boundary between acceptance and resignation?
  • How does the Stoic logos relate to modern natural laws? Both suggest a rationally ordered universe.
stoicisme-wat-je-wel-en-niet-kunt-beheer.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 28: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Epictetus, Enchiridion (freely available online)

symmetriebreking-waarom-imperfectie-de-w.titel: Symmetry Breaking — why imperfection creates the world
symmetriebreking-waarom-imperfectie-de-w.kern: The fundamental laws of nature are maximally symmetrical — they treat left and right equally, matter and antimatter equally, all directions equally. But the world we observe is not. All structure, all diversity, all matter exists because those symmetries were broken at some point. Imperfection is not a flaw in the system but the source of everything.
symmetriebreking-waarom-imperfectie-de-w.uitleg:
Noether's theorem proves that every symmetry yields a conservation law. The flip side is equally important: when a symmetry breaks, the corresponding conservation law disappears and something new appears — a particle, a force, a structure.

The most powerful example is the Higgs mechanism. In the first fraction of a second after the Big Bang, at temperatures of trillions of degrees, the electromagnetic force and the weak nuclear force were the same force — perfectly symmetrical. All particles were massless and moved at the speed of light. But as the universe cooled, the Higgs field "froze" into an asymmetric state. The symmetry broke, and the consequence was profound: some particles gained mass (the W and Z bosons became heavy), others did not (the photon remained massless). That is why electromagnetism and the weak force look so different today, even though they are the same force at the deepest level.

Without that symmetry breaking, there would be no mass, no atoms, no chemistry, no life. The world would consist of massless particles racing at the speed of light through a structureless universe.

Symmetry breaking is not limited to exotic particle physics. It appears everywhere:

A pencil balanced on its point. The law is symmetrical — there is no preference for which way it falls. But the pencil falls, and chooses one direction. The symmetry of the law is broken by the state.

Water that freezes. Liquid water looks the same in every direction. But an ice crystal has specific directions, specific structure. The higher symmetry of the liquid is broken, and the lower symmetry of the crystal appears — but precisely that lower symmetry makes structure possible.

Magnets. Above the Curie point, atomic moments point randomly — high symmetry, no magnetism. Below the Curie point, they align — broken symmetry, magnetism appears.

Matter versus antimatter. The laws are nearly perfectly symmetrical between the two. At the Big Bang, equal amounts were produced. But a minuscule asymmetry — one extra matter particle per billion pairs — ensured that after mutual annihilation, a tiny bit of matter remained. That remnant is all there is: stars, planets, you. Without that breaking, the universe would consist only of radiation. Why that asymmetry exists is one of the greatest open questions in physics.

Carroll sums it up: the laws of nature are a symphony of symmetry. But the world we inhabit is the result of that symphony playing false — in precisely the right ways.

The echo of Heraclitus is striking. Harmony from tension, order from breaking, structure from imperfection — it is the same intuition, expressed 2,500 years earlier without the mathematics but with the same core: reality exists because of its own asymmetries.
symmetriebreking-waarom-imperfectie-de-w.waarom:
It changes how you look at "perfection." A perfectly symmetrical universe would be empty and structureless — ultimate perfection is ultimate sterility. Everything that is interesting — matter, forces, life, consciousness — exists because perfection has been broken. Imperfection is not a deviation from the ideal but the condition for existence.

It also touches on Plato's theory of forms: Plato saw the imperfect world as a shadow of perfect Forms. Modern physics turns that around — the "perfect" symmetrical state is precisely the empty, uninteresting state. The shadows are richer than the light.
symmetriebreking-waarom-imperfectie-de-w.openVragen:
• If all symmetries of the Standard Model are broken, is there a deeper, yet unknown symmetry that unites them all? (Grand Unified Theory, supersymmetry?)
  • The matter/antimatter asymmetry remains unexplained. What would an explanation mean for our understanding of why the universe exists?
  • Are there analogues of symmetry breaking in biology? Is the choice of DNA for right-handed sugars (and not left-handed) a form of biological symmetry breaking?
symmetriebreking-waarom-imperfectie-de-w.verder_lezen:
• Carroll, Biggest Ideas #11 (Symmetry): https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #17 (Matter) — the Higgs mechanism and mass: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 2 (Basic Physics) — overview of forces and symmetries: https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_02.html
  • Al-Khalili, Atom (BBC Four, 2007, part 3: The Illusion of Reality) — symmetry and the structure of matter
  • Frank Close, The Infinity Puzzle (2011) — the history of the Standard Model and symmetry breaking

trofische-cascade.kern: What a top predator does at the top of a food web ripples downward in waves and reshapes the entire ecosystem — vegetation, soil, waterways, other animal species. Remove or add the top predator and the whole picture shifts.
trofische-cascade.uitleg:
The term was coined in 1980 by American ecologist Robert Paine, who had been conducting fieldwork on it since 1963 in tidal pools along the American Pacific coast. Paine experimentally removed starfish (the top predator) from bounded rock sections and observed what happened: mussels, freed from predation, overgrew everything. Other species — snails, barnacles, algae — disappeared. Biodiversity plummeted. One species, threading through the entire web, determined the fate of many.

The most famous example came decades later. In 1995, wolves were reintroduced to Yellowstone after seventy years of absence. Elk had previously been able to graze undisturbed — lingering in one place and trimming willows, cottonwoods, and aspens. With wolves present, elk became cautious: they avoided valleys and riverbanks where they could be cornered. There the trees recovered. Beavers — which needed the willows — returned and built dams. Dams created pools, pools created fish, bird, and amphibian habitat. The rivers themselves changed course, because vegetated banks eroded less. The entire valley was reorganized by a handful of wolves.

In England, Isabella Tree and Charlie Burrell did something similar on the Knepp Estate without wolves. They allowed 1,400 hectares of farmland to rewild, with large grazers (heritage cattle breeds, ponies, deer, pigs) as ecosystem architects. The free movement of those grazers created a mosaic of thorny scrub forests, open meadows, and wetlands — habitat for rare species such as turtle doves, purple emperors, and nightingales that had disappeared elsewhere in England. Tree describes this story in Wilding (2018), one of the most influential rewilding books of the past decade.

What trophic cascades show us is that ecosystems don't need to be managed from above with a human hand. Restore the right links — a top predator, a large grazer, a beaver — and the system regulates itself in ways we often cannot predict.
trofische-cascade.waarom: Trophic cascades operate at every scale, even in a garden. Controlling slugs with poison sounds logical but also poisons the hedgehogs, thrushes, and frogs that eat slugs — and without those predators you get a slug explosion instead. The same pattern: a top predator (hedgehog, cat, thrush) at the top keeps the system in balance. Remove it and you get more pests, not fewer. Thinking in cascades forces you to look beyond the direct intervention — to the entire web of which your garden is a part.
trofische-cascade.openVragen:
• How do you identify which species is the top predator of a local ecosystem before you disturb it?
  • Do trophic cascades also work from the bottom up — can you reshape an ecosystem by adding an important plant or bacterium?
trofische-cascade.verder_lezen:
• Tree, Wilding (2018) — the Knepp story in book form
  • Knepp Estate website: knepp.co.uk — virtual safaris and background
  • George Monbiot, "How wolves change rivers" — four minutes on Yellowstone, beautifully edited
  • Paine, "Food Web Complexity and Species Diversity" (1966) — the original paper

van-potentie-naar-act-superpositie-symme.titel: From Potentiality to Actuality — Superposition, Symmetry Breaking, and the Birth of Reality
van-potentie-naar-act-superpositie-symme.kern: Both quantum superposition and symmetry breaking describe the same fundamental transition: from all possibilities at once to one realized reality. Aristotle had a word for this 2,400 years ago — the transition from potentiality to actuality — and this makes it one of the deepest intersections between philosophy and modern physics.
van-potentie-naar-act-superpositie-symme.uitleg:
Aristotle introduced the conceptual pair dynamis (potentiality, possibility) and energeia (actuality, reality) as a solution to an ancient problem. Parmenides had argued that change is logically impossible: something cannot arise from Being (for then it already existed) or from Non-Being (for nothing comes from nothing). Aristotle's answer: there is an intermediate state. An acorn is not an oak, but it is also not "nothing" — it is an oak in potentiality. Change is the transition from potentiality to actuality.

Russell treats this as one of Aristotle's most important contributions: it makes change thinkable without falling into Parmenides' trap. But Russell also criticizes the vagueness — what exactly is "potentiality"? It sounds as though it is a property of things, but is it not rather a property of our description?

Remarkably, modern physics has two phenomena that realize the potentiality-actuality structure almost literally.

Quantum superposition is the first. An electron in the double-slit experiment exists before measurement in a superposition — it is not "at slit A" or "at slit B" but in a state that encompasses all possibilities at once. This is not a lack of knowledge (we do not know where it is), but the nature of reality at the quantum scale: the particle is all possibilities at once. It is pure potentiality, in Aristotelian terms.

Upon measurement, the wavefunction collapses to one outcome. The particle appears at slit A or slit B. Potentiality becomes actuality. But the process is mysterious — no one knows precisely how or why the "choice" is made. This is the measurement problem, one of the deepest open questions in physics.

Symmetry breaking is the second phenomenon, and it operates on a different scale. The Higgs field in the early universe had no preference — all directions in the abstract field space were equivalent. The system was symmetric: all possibilities existed in potentiality. When the universe cooled, the field "chose" one direction. The symmetry broke, and the world we know — with mass, with different forces, with structure — appeared. Potentiality became actuality.

The analogy is tempting, but there are crucial differences. In quantum superposition, all possibilities exist actually at once — this is no metaphor. In symmetry breaking, all possibilities existed as equivalent options, but the choice is definitive and thermodynamic: the system falls to the lowest energy state, which happens to be asymmetric. No observer is needed — only cooling.

Another way to see the difference: in quantum collapse (in the standard interpretation), the unchosen possibilities truly vanish. In symmetry breaking, they remain — hidden in the mathematics of the laws. The pencil could have fallen the other way. The symmetry is not gone but hidden.

Yet both processes share a deeper structure: the transition from a state with maximal possibilities (high symmetry, superposition) to a state with fewer possibilities but more structure (broken symmetry, measured outcome). And that is precisely what Aristotle meant: reality is what remains when possibilities narrow to one.

Carroll extends this line in Biggest Ideas toward cosmology. The universe began in a state of extremely low entropy — high order, but also high symmetry. Everything was equal, uniform, undifferentiated. The history of the universe is the story of continuous symmetry breaking: first the fundamental forces splitting, then matter clumping (gravity and local order), then stars forming, then planets, then life. Each step is a transition from potentiality to actuality — from more possibilities to fewer possibilities but more structure.

Heraclitus would recognize it: the world is eternal becoming, not static being. And Parmenides would be uncomfortable: the fundamental laws (his unchanging Being) are still there, but the reality that flows from them is in perpetual motion. Aristotle's synthesis — potentiality and actuality as two modes of being — turns out, after 2,400 years, to have the structure that modern physics needed.
van-potentie-naar-act-superpositie-symme.waarom: It is the deepest intersection in the entire vault: a line running from the oldest philosophical question (how is change possible?) through modern quantum mechanics (superposition and measurement) to cosmology (symmetry breaking and the structure of the universe). It shows that philosophy and physics are not two separate disciplines but two languages for the same problem: how does possibility become reality?
van-potentie-naar-act-superpositie-symme.openVragen:
• Is quantum collapse a form of symmetry breaking? Some interpretations (decoherence, many-worlds) suggest it is — but there is no consensus.
  • Did Aristotle have a point that modern physics misses? His potentiality is a property of the thing itself, not of our description. Quantum mechanics is ambiguous on this question — is the wavefunction a property of reality or of our knowledge?
  • If the universe is a series of symmetry breakings, was the original symmetric state "more real" or "less real" than the broken state we inhabit?
van-potentie-naar-act-superpositie-symme.verder_lezen:
• Russell, History of Western Philosophy, Book I Ch. 19-22 — Aristotle's potentiality and actuality: https://www.ntslibrary.com/PDF%20Books/History%20of%20Western%20Philosophy.pdf
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #13 (Quantum Mechanics) — superposition and measurement: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #11 (Symmetry) — symmetry breaking: https://www.preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas/videos/
  • Feynman, Six Easy Pieces, Piece 6 (Quantum Behavior) — the double-slit experiment as pure potentiality: https://www.feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/I_37.html
  • Al-Khalili, Quantum: A Guide for the Perplexed (2003) — the transition from superposition to measurement
  • Carlo Rovelli, Reality Is Not What It Seems (2014) — quantum gravity as the ultimate synthesis of potentiality and actuality

water-als-levensader-in-de-tuin.titel: Water as the Lifeblood of the Garden
water-als-levensader-in-de-tuin.kern: A water feature — even a shallow basin or a half-barrel — is the single element that increases a garden's biodiversity most rapidly and dramatically. Water attracts life that simply would not exist without it.
water-als-levensader-in-de-tuin.uitleg:
Within weeks of placing a pond or even a large container of water, inhabitants appear that you did not invite: water striders (beetles that skate across the water's surface), damselflies, dragonflies, backswimmers, and in the evenings amphibians drawn by the sound of water. This is no accident — water is the missing element in most gardens, and many animal species depend entirely on it for at least one phase of their life cycle.

Dragonflies and damselflies lay their eggs in or near water. Their larvae live for one to three years underwater as formidable predators (they eat mosquito larvae — a pleasant side effect). The common toad, brown frog, and small newt require a pond to mate and lay eggs, but spend most of the year on land, in damp corners with leaf litter and wood piles.

Birds need water to drink and bathe. A shallow edge (maximum 5 cm deep) with a rough surface is ideal — birds are vulnerable while bathing and want to escape quickly. A sloping bank or a stone just above the water makes all the difference.

Even the plants around a pond are ecologically valuable: marsh forget-me-not, cattail, watercress, and reed mace offer nectar to pollinators and shelter to insects.

The parallel with Thales is fortuitous but fitting: water as the primordial principle, as that from which life emerges. In the context of a garden, this is almost literally true — add water and life appears.

Practical tips: avoid fish in a small pond (they consume all larvae and eggs), use rainwater rather than tap water (chlorine), ensure planted edges and a deep section (at least 60 cm) so amphibians can overwinter without freezing.
water-als-levensader-in-de-tuin.waarom: A garden without water is like a city without parks: it can exist, but a whole dimension is missing. Water does not add a few species but an entire ecological guild — amphibians, dragonflies, aquatic macroinvertebrates — that is simply absent without it.
water-als-levensader-in-de-tuin.openVragen:
• How small can a water feature be to remain ecologically meaningful? Is a bucket sufficient for mosquito larvae-eating aquatic inhabitants?
  • How do you manage the inevitable algal bloom in the first year?
water-als-levensader-in-de-tuin.verder_lezen:
• Dave Goulson, The Garden Jungle (2019) — the effect of water on garden biodiversity
  • Darwin, The Formation of Vegetable Mould (1881) — the relationship between moist soil and earthworm activity

het-wood-wide-web.kern: Beneath the soil of every forest lies a network of fungal threads that connects trees to one another. Through this network, trees exchange sugars, nutrients, and even warning signals — an underground internet of the forest.
het-wood-wide-web.uitleg:
The concept of mycorrhiza describes the symbiosis between a single plant and a single fungal network. But mycorrhizal fungi are not limited to one tree. Their mycelium threads connect to the roots of multiple trees simultaneously, sometimes dozens, often of different species. The result is a network that spans the entire forest.

In Entangled Life, Sheldrake describes how carbon (sugars) can flow through these networks from a tree that catches abundant sunlight to a tree in the shade. Mother trees — large, old trees with extensive root systems — appear to nourish their seedlings via the fungal network, giving young trees a higher chance of survival.

Even more remarkable: when a tree is attacked by insects, it can emit chemical signals that travel through the mycelium to neighboring trees. Those trees then begin to produce defensive substances preemptively, before the insects reach them. The forest responds as a collective, not as a collection of individuals.

The term "wood wide web" is popular but not uncontroversial in science. Sheldrake himself is cautious: the metaphor of an "internet" suggests conscious communication, whereas in reality it involves chemical processes. The fungus is not an altruistic messenger but an organism serving its own interests — it delivers nutrients to trees that give it sugars in return. The "communication" is a byproduct of that transaction.

Yet it fundamentally changes how we view a forest: not as an arena where trees compete for light, but as a community connected underground by a fungal network that can be older than the trees themselves.
het-wood-wide-web.waarom: It shifts perspective from individual to network. A tree is not an autonomous organism but a node in a web of relationships. That echoes Heraclitus: everything is connected, and the connection is more real than the separate parts.
het-wood-wide-web.openVragen:
• To what extent does this network also function in a garden with fruit trees and shrubs, or is it primarily a forest phenomenon?
  • If the fungus manages the network, who holds the "power" — the tree or the fungus?
het-wood-wide-web.verder_lezen:
• Sheldrake, Entangled Life, Ch. 6 (Wood Wide Webs): https://archive.org/details/entangled-life_202203
  • Suzanne Simard, Finding the Mother Tree (2021) — the ecologist who discovered the wood wide web

het-curie-punt.kern: Below a certain temperature, electron spins in ferromagnetic materials like iron spontaneously align and magnetism emerges. Above it, they point randomly and the material loses its magnetism. This critical temperature is called the Curie point — and it is one of the most beautiful everyday examples of spontaneous symmetry breaking.
het-curie-punt.uitleg:
Pierre Curie discovered the transition in 1895, long before quantum theory could explain it. He observed that iron suddenly loses its magnetic properties at 770 °C — not gradually, but like a switch flipping. Above that threshold, a magnet is no longer a magnet.

The modern explanation lies in electron spins. Each electron is a tiny magnet, and in ferromagnetic materials, neighbouring spins can reward each other energetically via the so-called exchange interaction when they point in the same direction. Below the Curie point, this ordering force wins out against the thermal motion that would otherwise shake the spins randomly. The system spontaneously chooses one direction — which direction doesn't matter, but once the choice is made, it is fixed and the whole block of iron acquires a net magnetization. Above the Curie point, thermal chaos wins and the spins point in all directions at once: on average, zero magnetization.

Not every metal has a Curie point. It is reserved for the four ferromagnetic elements — iron (770 °C), cobalt (1115 °C), nickel (358 °C), gadolinium (19 °C) — plus a handful of alloys and oxides (magnetite, Alnico, neodymium magnets). Copper, aluminium, gold and silver are not ferromagnetic and thus have no Curie point. Antiferromagnetic materials like chromium have an analogous phase transition at their Néel temperature: here neighbouring spins align in opposite directions instead, in an ordered checkerboard pattern.

Curie temperatures vary wildly because the exchange interaction depends on the specific crystal lattice and electron configuration. Gadolinium loses its magnetism at room temperature — a gadolinium magnet in your hand switches itself "off". Cobalt, by contrast, remains magnetic until nearly 1100 °C, making it indispensable for permanent magnets in high-temperature applications.

What makes the physics here so elegant is that the Curie point is a phase transition in the same mathematical sense as melting or boiling. Above Tc, the system has rotational symmetry — all directions are equivalent. Below Tc, the system chooses one and spontaneously breaks that symmetry. This is the same pattern that recurs in superconductivity (where electrons below a critical temperature choose to form pairs), in liquid helium entering a superfluid state, and in a much deeper sense in the Higgs mechanism that gives particles their mass. The Curie point is thus not a curiosity of solid-state physics but the most tangible instance of a principle that reaches to the foundations of particle physics.
het-curie-punt.waarom: Most people know magnetism as a static property — an iron magnet is magnetic, full stop. But magnetism is actually a phase like the liquid-solid-gas phases of water, and you can switch that phase in and out with temperature. Heat a magnet above 770 °C and it is just a piece of glowing metal; cool it down and the magnetism returns by itself — but the direction of that new magnetization is random, unless you apply another magnetic field. That is exactly how permanent magnets are "made" in factories: heated in a strong external field, so they choose the right direction as they cool through their Curie point. The physics of a refrigerator magnet is thus literally symmetry breaking in action.
het-curie-punt.openVragen:
• Why do precisely iron, cobalt, nickel and gadolinium have a ferromagnetic ground state while their neighbouring elements in the periodic table do not?
  • Is there a fundamental connection between the symmetry breaking at the Curie point and the symmetry breaking that gives particles their mass via the Higgs — or is the mathematical resemblance superficial?
het-curie-punt.verder_lezen:
• Feynman, Lectures on Physics, Vol II Ch. 36-37 — ferromagnetism and the exchange interaction: feynmanlectures.caltech.edu/II_36.html
  • Carroll, Biggest Ideas #18 (Symmetry): preposterousuniverse.com/biggestideas
  • HyperPhysics, "Ferromagnetism" — clear reference with formulas
  • Wikipedia, "Curie temperature" — table of Tc values for all known ferromagnets
